## Hardware Requirements

| Field | Value |
|---|---|
| **URL** | https://blendata.atlassian.net/wiki/spaces/bde460/pages/2202730550/Hardware+Requirements |
| **Last modified** | 2026-05-28T15:52:21.345Z |
| **Breadcrumb** | Blendata Enterprise V.4.6.0 > Administrator User Manual > Blendata Specification |

This section specifies the hardware requirements for Blendata Enterprise.

When designing your cluster architecture, you must allocate system resources appropriately across different modules to ensure optimal performance and scalability.

If multiple modules are deployed on the same host, the total CPU, memory, and storage requirements of all assigned modules must be combined to determine the required hardware capacity.

Blendata Enterprise provides predefined hardware sizing models, including a Minimum Design and a Recommended Production Design, to guide infrastructure planning.

### Minimum Design (POC / Standalone Deployment)

High Availability (HA) and redundancy are not supported in this configuration.

|  |  |  |  |  |  |
| --- | --- | --- | --- | --- | --- |
| **Component** | **vCPU** | **Memory (GB)** | **Disk (GB)** | **No. of instances** | **Note** |
| Web and Management | 8 | 24 | >250 | 1 |  |
| Backend service | 8 | 24 | >250 | 1 |  |
| Application and Compute for DWH  OR  Application and Compute for DE | 32   ---   24 | 256   ---   64 | >590 | 1 |  |
| Workflow (Optional) | 8 | 32 | >290 | 1 |  |
| Data Science Notebook(Optional) | 8 | 32 | >550 | 1 |  |

### Recommended Design

|  |  |  |  |  |  |
| --- | --- | --- | --- | --- | --- |
| **Component** | **vCPU** | **Memory (GB)** | **Disk (GB)** | **No. of instances** | **Note** |
| Management | 2 | 8 | >250 | 1 |  |
| Web | 12 | 32 | >250 | 2 |  |
| Backend service | 12 | 32 | >250 | 3 |  |
| Application (HPC Application) | 16 | 64 | >250 | 1\* |  |
| Worker | 12 | 128 | >590 | 1\* | Larger disk capacity is required due to Spark scratch space usage (see Disk and Storage Considerations below). |
| Workflow | 8 | 48 | >290 | 1 |  |
| Data Science Notebook | 16 | 64 | >550 | 1\* |  |

**1\* = Application (HPC Application)**, **Worker**, and **Data Science Notebook** components require at least one dedicated node. Additional nodes may be provisioned based on workload and scalability requirements.

**Disk and Storage Considerations (Worker Nodes)**

The Worker disk sizing includes Spark local scratch space used for shuffle and spill operations.

- Requires scratch space: 2–5x memory size.
- SSD or NVMe storage is strongly recommended.
- Consider mounting a separate disk for Spark local directories (e.g., `/tmp` or `spark.local.dir`).

### **Component-to-Host Mapping**

The following table describes how system services are distributed across different host roles in the recommended production deployment model.

|  |  |  |  |  |  |
| --- | --- | --- | --- | --- | --- |
| **Web and Management** | **Backend service** | **Application and Compute** | **Worker** | **Workflow (Optional)** | **Data Science Notebook(Optional)** |
| BDE management | Database metastore (MySQL/PostgreSQL) | HPC Application  (one or multiples) | Spark worker | Workflow statsd + Agent | Zeppelin server |
| Hera | Zookeeper (for HA spark master) | BDE management agent | BDE management agent | Workflow Scheduler | Zeppelin python interpreter (Python,Spark,Java) |
| Cerberus | Spark master | BDE agent |  | Workflow redis | BDE management agent |
| Notification | Spark history |  |  | Workflow agent (by BDE) | BDE agent |
| BDE management agent | BDE management agent |  |  | Workflow trigger |  |
| BDE agent | BDE agent |  |  | Workflow worker |  |
|  | Redis service |  |  | Workflow web + Scheduler + Trigger |  |
|  |  |  |  | BDE management agent |  |
|  |  |  |  | BDE agent |  |

### Sample Deployment Configuration

| **Internal IP** | **Role** | **Component** | **vCPU (Cores) / Memory (GB)** |
| --- | --- | --- | --- |
| 1xx.xx.xx.xxx | Web Module | - Blendata management (Genesis) (8xxxx)  - Management Agent (12xxx)  - Cerberus (11xxx)  - Hera (11xxx)  - NotiServer (10xxx)  - Brown-Cow (11xxx) | 12 Cores / 32 GB |
| 2xx.xx.xx.xxx | Backend Module | - Management Agent (12xxx)  - Brown-Cow (11xxx)  - PostgreSQL for BDE (54xx)  - Spark Master (80xx)  - Spark History (18xxx) | 8 Cores / 24 GB |
| 3xx.xx.xx.xxx | Notebook & Workflow | - Management Agent (12xxx)  - Brown-Cow (11xxx)  - ZEPPELIN (89xx)  - PostgreSQL (54xx)  - Airflow (12xxx)  - Workflow Management (40xx) | 8 Cores / 24 GB |
| 4xx.xx.xx.xxx | Worker Node | - Management Agent (12xxx)  - Brown-Cow (11xxx)  - Spark Worker (80xx)  - ZEUS\_I (40x0, 11xxx, 99xx) | 12 Cores / 166 GB |
| 5xx.xx.xx.xxx | Worker Node | - Management Agent (12xxx)  - Brown-Cow (11xxx)  - Spark Worker (80xx)  - ZEUS\_II (40x1, 11xxx, 99xx) | 12 Cores / 166 GB |
| 6xx.xx.xx.xxx | NAS | Network Attached Storage | 4 Cores / 8 GB |

**Note:** The configurations listed above are intended for demonstration/reference purposes only. Proper resource planning and sizing must be conducted prior to production deployment.

<https://blendata.atlassian.net/wiki/spaces/bde460/pages/2202730498>

[**Next: →**](https://blendata.atlassian.net/wiki/spaces/bde460/pages/2977333251/Supported+Operating+Systems)

---

## Tuning & optimization

| Field | Value |
|---|---|
| **URL** | https://blendata.atlassian.net/wiki/spaces/bde460/pages/2202731320/Tuning+optimization |
| **Last modified** | 2025-01-17T09:21:57.099Z |
| **Breadcrumb** | Blendata Enterprise V.4.6.0 > General References |

[data-colorid=jrrrp7a4jv]{color:#006644} html[data-color-mode=dark] [data-colorid=jrrrp7a4jv]{color:#99ffdd} **Blendata-Enterprise**, it consists of various modules. They mostly run on JVM and the main engine developed on APACHE SPARK technology. Will mostly interact between modules with RestAPI. Thus, this article describes the performance tuning and optimization for each part.

# JVM Tuning

To tuning overall performance of the JVM, there are two important parts, Heap memory and Garbage Collector (GC).

## 1. Heap memory

To allocated memory by [setting the heap size](https://docs.oracle.com/cd/E13150_01/jrockit_jvm/jrockit/geninfo/diagnos/memman.html#wp1090168), we recommended **-Xmx** paramter to define the maximum heap sizes for module tuning.

Default maximum heap sizes and a conditions for increasing the heap sizes in each modules.

| **Module Name** | **-Xmx** | **Script file path for customization** | **A conditions for increasing the heap sizes** |
| --- | --- | --- | --- |
| Cerberus | 2 GB | cerberus\bin\start-cerberus.sh | Found out-of-memory error in application logs. |
| Hera | 4 GB | hera\bin\start-hera.sh | When Herastacks many job processes in queue or found out-of-memory error in application logs. |
| Zeus (Spark Driver) | 2 GB | zeus\bin\spark.conf | When Zeus swap disk space to use on memory. |
| Zeus (Spark Excecuter) | 32 GB | zeus\bin\spark.conf | When Zeus swap disk space to use on memory. |
| Brow Cow | 2 GB | brown-cow\bin\start-brow-cow.sh | Found out-of-memory error in application logs. |

*Spark Executer memory config related to* [*Spark Tuning*](https://g-templar.atlassian.net/wiki/spaces/BE/pages/1377173505/Tuning+optimization#Spark-Tuning)

## 2. Garbage Collector (GC)

A garbage collector performs automatic dynamic memory management by related with Heap memory size. JVM has four garbage collector implementations:

- Serial GC
- Parallel GC
- CMS GC
- G1 GC

ZEUS module use G1 GC by default, supported large memories management. For other modules will use Parallel GC by default.

# Spark Tuning

In this section, we'll talk about the topics on **memory tuning, memory management, and garbage collection tuning**. Due to other SPARK tuning topics, we have default configurations that the typical user should not need to adjust them as the default values are applicable to most workloads.

## Memory Tuning

There are three considerations in tuning memory usage: the amount of memory used by your objects (you may want your entire dataset to fit in memory), the cost of accessing those objects, and the overhead of garbage collection (if you have high turnover in terms of objects).

By default, Java objects are fast to access, but can easily consume a factor of 2-5x more space than the “raw” data inside their fields. This is due to several reasons:

- Each distinct Java object has an “object header”, which is about 16 bytes and contains information such as a pointer to its class. For an object with very little data in it (say one `Int` field), this can be bigger than the data.
- Java `String`s have about 40 bytes of overhead over the raw string data (since they store it in an array of `Char`s and keep extra data such as the length), and store each character as *two* bytes due to `String`’s internal usage of UTF-16 encoding. Thus a 10-character string can easily consume 60 bytes.
- Common collection classes, such as `HashMap` and `LinkedList`, use linked data structures, where there is a “wrapper” object for each entry (e.g. `Map.Entry`). This object not only has a header, but also pointers (typically 8 bytes each) to the next object in the list.
- Collections of primitive types often store them as “boxed” objects such as `java.lang.Integer`.

This section will start with an overview of memory management in Spark, then discuss specific strategies the user can take to make more efficient use of memory in his/her application. In particular, we will describe how to determine the memory usage of your objects, and how to improve it – either by changing your data structures, or by storing data in a serialized format. We will then cover tuning Spark’s cache size and the Java garbage collector.

## Memory Management

Memory usage in Spark largely falls under one of two categories: execution and storage. Execution memory refers to that used for computation in shuffles, joins, sorts and aggregations, while storage memory refers to that used for caching and propagating internal data across the cluster. In Spark, execution and storage share a unified region (M). When no execution memory is used, storage can acquire all the available memory and vice versa. Execution may evict storage if necessary, but only until total storage memory usage falls under a certain threshold (R). In other words, `R` describes a subregion within `M` where cached blocks are never evicted. Storage may not evict execution due to complexities in implementation.

This design ensures several desirable properties. First, applications that do not use caching can use the entire space for execution, obviating unnecessary disk spills. Second, applications that do use caching can reserve a minimum storage space (R) where their data blocks are immune to being evicted. Lastly, this approach provides reasonable out-of-the-box performance for a variety of workloads without requiring user expertise of how memory is divided internally.

Although there are two relevant configurations, the typical user should not need to adjust them as the default values are applicable to most workloads:

- `spark.memory.fraction` expresses the size of `M` as a fraction of the (JVM heap space - 300MB) (default 0.6). The rest of the space (40%) is reserved for user data structures, internal metadata in Spark, and safeguarding against OOM errors in the case of sparse and unusually large records.
- `spark.memory.storageFraction` expresses the size of `R` as a fraction of `M` (default 0.5). `R` is the storage space within `M` where cached blocks immune to being evicted by execution.

The value of `spark.memory.fraction` should be set in order to fit this amount of heap space comfortably within the JVM’s old or “tenured” generation. See the discussion of advanced GC tuning below for details.

## Garbage Collection Tuning

JVM garbage collection can be a problem when you have large “churn” in terms of the RDDs stored by your program. (It is usually not a problem in programs that just read an RDD once and then run many operations on it.) When Java needs to evict old objects to make room for new ones, it will need to trace through all your Java objects and find the unused ones. The main point to remember here is that *the cost of garbage collection is proportional to the number of Java objects*, so using data structures with fewer objects (e.g. an array of `Int`s instead of a `LinkedList`) greatly lowers this cost. An even better method is to persist objects in serialized form, as described above: now there will be only *one* object (a byte array) per RDD partition. Before trying other techniques, the first thing to try if GC is a problem is to use serialized caching.

GC can also be a problem due to interference between your tasks’ working memory (the amount of space needed to run the task) and the RDDs cached on your nodes. We will discuss how to control the space allocated to the RDD cache to mitigate this.

### **Measuring the Impact of GC**

The first step in GC tuning is to collect statistics on how frequently garbage collection occurs and the amount of time spent GC. This can be done by adding `-verbose:gc -XX:+PrintGCDetails -XX:+PrintGCTimeStamps` to the Java options. (See the spark configuration guide for info on passing Java options to Spark jobs.) Next time your Spark job is run, you will see messages printed in the worker’s logs each time a garbage collection occurs. Note these logs will be on your cluster’s worker nodes (in the `stdout` files in their work directories), *not* on your driver program.

### **Advanced GC Tuning**

To further tune garbage collection, we first need to understand some basic information about memory management in the JVM:

- Java Heap space is divided in to two regions Young and Old. The Young generation is meant to hold short-lived objects while the Old generation is intended for objects with longer lifetimes.
- The Young generation is further divided into three regions [Eden, Survivor1, Survivor2].
- A simplified description of the garbage collection procedure: When Eden is full, a minor GC is run on Eden and objects that are alive from Eden and Survivor1 are copied to Survivor2. The Survivor regions are swapped. If an object is old enough or Survivor2 is full, it is moved to Old. Finally, when Old is close to full, a full GC is invoked.

The goal of GC tuning in Spark is to ensure that only long-lived RDDs are stored in the Old generation and that the Young generation is sufficiently sized to store short-lived objects. This will help avoid full GCs to collect temporary objects created during task execution. Some steps which may be useful are:

- Check if there are too many garbage collections by collecting GC stats. If a full GC is invoked multiple times for before a task completes, it means that there isn’t enough memory available for executing tasks.
- If there are too many minor collections but not many major GCs, allocating more memory for Eden would help. You can set the size of the Eden to be an over-estimate of how much memory each task will need. If the size of Eden is determined to be `E`, then you can set the size of the Young generation using the option `-Xmn=4/3*E`. (The scaling up by 4/3 is to account for space used by survivor regions as well.)
- In the GC stats that are printed, if the OldGen is close to being full, reduce the amount of memory used for caching by lowering `spark.memory.fraction`; it is better to cache fewer objects than to slow down task execution. Alternatively, consider decreasing the size of the Young generation. This means lowering `-Xmn` if you’ve set it as above. If not, try changing the value of the JVM’s `NewRatio` parameter. Many JVMs default this to 2, meaning that the Old generation occupies 2/3 of the heap. It should be large enough such that this fraction exceeds `spark.memory.fraction`.
- Try the G1GC garbage collector with `-XX:+UseG1GC`. It can improve performance in some situations where garbage collection is a bottleneck. Note that with large executor heap sizes, it may be important to increase the G1 region size with `-XX:G1HeapRegionSize`
- As an example, if your task is reading data from HDFS, the amount of memory used by the task can be estimated using the size of the data block read from HDFS. Note that the size of a decompressed block is often 2 or 3 times the size of the block. So if we wish to have 3 or 4 tasks’ worth of working space, and the HDFS block size is 128 MB, we can estimate size of Eden to be `4*3*128MB`.
- Monitor how the frequency and time taken by garbage collection changes with the new settings.

Our experience suggests that the effect of GC tuning depends on your application and the amount of memory available. There are many more tuning options described online, but at a high level, managing how frequently full GC takes place can help in reducing the overhead.

GC tuning flags for executors can be specified by setting `spark.executor.extraJavaOptions` in a job’s configuration.

*Credit by:* [*Apache Spark*](https://spark.apache.org/docs/2.4.7/tuning.html)

From our experience of Big Data Analytics Most of the time, the minimum data size used each time is **100 GB**. Therefore, we calculate the basic minimum requirements to work with this size.

**Minimum Recommended**: ***8 core 64 GB per execute \* 3 nodes*** for handle data ***100 GB***

- We recommend using swap 4-6 times and should not exceed 10 times.
- Storage can use up to 50% of the shared memory space by default configuration. In which the shared memory area accounts for 60% of the JVM heap memory by default configuration.
- In general, Spark can run well with anywhere from 8 GB to hundreds of gigabytes of memory per machine.
- The Java VM does not always behave well with more than 200 GB of RAM
- We recommend allocating only at most 75% of the memory for Spark

You can find more information in the [Apache Spark Reference.](https://spark.apache.org/docs/latest/tuning.html)

# Job Process Tuning

Hera embeds Quartz scheduler for Job management server. All jobs and scheduler are stored in the database and can be configured by properties in file `quartz.properties` as described in [Hera Configuration](https://blendata.atlassian.net/wiki/spaces/BE1/pages/1895433001) section Quartz Properties. The parameter you should focus on optimization is `org.quartz.threadPool.threadCount` which is the number of maximum concurrent execution of jobs. For example, if jobs are scheduled to execute at the same time 100 jobs. Setting `org.quartz.threadPool.threadCount` to 100, all jobs can be executed at exactly the specific time. While setting to less than 100, there will be the remaining jobs waiting in the queues. However, configure this value too high leads to high resource usages, configure this value too low may cause some jobs not to be executed. Therefore, you should set an appropriate value based on the users' behavior in the system.  
  
`quartz.properties`

```
# thread-pool
org.quartz.threadPool.class=org.quartz.simpl.SimpleThreadPool
org.quartz.threadPool.threadCount=20
org.quartz.threadPool.threadsInheritContextClassLoaderOfInitializingThread=true

# Enable these properties for a JDBCJobStore using JobStoreTX
org.quartz.jobStore.class=org.quartz.impl.jdbcjobstore.JobStoreTX
org.quartz.jobStore.driverDelegateClass=org.quartz.impl.jdbcjobstore.StdJDBCDelegate
org.quartz.jobStore.dataSource=quartzDataSource
org.quartz.jobStore.misfireThreshold = 1000
```

# Response Tuning

As depicted in [Architecture components overview](https://blendata.atlassian.net/wiki/spaces/bdth/pages/1816726866/Architecture+Component+overview), Blendata composes of 3 core modules; Cerberus, Hera, Zeus. Hera connects to Cerberus service for security management and connects to Zeus for Spark service processing. Thus, one of the important considerations for module communication is web service timeout. This timeout can be configured by the following properties.

## **Hera**

`config/application.properties`

```
# Web service calling timeout to Cerberus,Zeus modules
http.connect.timeout = 86400000
http.read.timeout = 86400000
```

```
# Web service calling timeout to Hera for Job management e.g. Refresh Job, Recovery Job, etc.
job.service.http.connect.timeout = 86400000
job.service.http.read.timeout = 86400000
```

Too high timeout value meaning the process will hold resource usages and not release for a long time. While too low timeout value may cause the service to mistakenly respond and inconsistent data synchronization.

# Data Storage Optimization

Blendata-Enterprise It stores data in the Parquet file format and has a data policy for handling each state data, meaning Parquet can reduce data size from the raw by up to 87%[1] . as well as can be compressed the parquet files, and you can choose compression type in each state of data with the [configuration of Hera module](https://blendata.atlassian.net/wiki/spaces/BE1/pages/1895433001), eg. Snappy, GZIP, etc., so can further reduce the size of the file according to the compression type.

Each state configuration name is as below.

> data.state.hot.compression  
> data.state.warm.compression  
> data.state.cold.compression

***Appendix***

[1] *:* [*Reference to Apache Parquet*](https://databricks.com/glossary/what-is-parquet)

---

## Performance Tuning Tips

| Field | Value |
|---|---|
| **URL** | https://blendata.atlassian.net/wiki/spaces/bde460/pages/2202731347/Performance+Tuning+Tips |
| **Last modified** | 2025-01-17T09:21:57.315Z |
| **Breadcrumb** | Blendata Enterprise V.4.6.0 > General References > Tuning & optimization |

Latest Update: September 6, 2024

# Regarding Storage

## 1. Partition Your Data

Partitioning divides your table data into sections, storing related information together based on column values, such as date, country, or region. The partition acts as a "virtual column."

Benefits**:** When you query data with a filter that matches a partition, such as filtering by date, the system scans only the relevant partition. This speeds up queries since it doesn't need to scan the entire table.

**Considerations for Designing Partitions:**

1. Choose a partition key that aligns with your query patterns, as queries will refer back to the fields used in data filtering.
2. The partition key should not have too many unique values. For example, a partition key with only a few possible values, like "country" with just a few dozen countries, has "low cardinality." Conversely, a key like "CustomerID" in a large customer database could have millions of unique values, leading to high cardinality. This could result in a large number of partitions, increasing the database's overhead as it manages metadata for each partition.
3. If your data is heavily skewed towards one partition value and most queries use that value, it may create overhead and reduce the benefits of partitioning.

**Note**: Not having partitions can lead to high overhead as if the data wasn't divided at all. However, having too many partitions can also cause overhead due to the large number of small files. Therefore, careful consideration is needed during the design phase. If your data volume is small, partitioning might not be necessary.

**Blendata: Data Catalog Menu or Import Data in the "Create Table Option" Step**

![](https://blendata.atlassian.net/wiki/download/attachments/2202731347/AD_4nXehlsoeDVtvZOdEKknCLsoNCxoWOsif7zVrSgYhfsJcRtBBCy6crxucWKRDUNV1UYtkpQjuXCIEJ_WK3JocS26dhfxicSl1rjxfveg_emFpmZDh-K_qI0pDVyUgz7bwk1iCKz9jz4UAkIQMZjnOTFhlxghd%3Fkey=hfxOw1K9Db4wqcNNMo9HFA?api=v2)

For time-series tables, the system will automatically create a partition\_date based on the time field selected by the user. Additionally, users can add hierarchical levels to partition time series data. The available partition options include:

- [Generate] Year (yyyy)
- [Generate] Month (yyyyMM)
- [Generate] Hour (yyyyMMddHH)
- [Generate] Minute (yyyyMMddHHmm)

Usage: When querying in tables, you can limit partitions by using the WHERE clause in your statement, for example:

The dataset, approximately 306 MB in size, is used to compare query runtimes and Parquet scan performance between partitioned and non-partitioned tables.

|  |  |  |  |
| --- | --- | --- | --- |
| **Query** | **Non - Partitioned Table** | **Partitioned Table** | **Comparison** |
| SELECT COUNT(\*)  FROM pakistan\_e\_commerce  WHERE year >= 2017 AND      (category\_name\_1 = 'Mobiles & Tablets'  OR category\_name\_1 =   "Men's Fashion")' | **Runtime**: 3.9 seconds   ---   **Scan parquet**  size of files read: 308.0 MiB  Number of Output Rows: 13,290,528 rows | **Runtime:** 3.6 seconds   ---   **Scan parquet**  size of files read: 66.4 MiB  Number of Output Rows:  3,844,009 rows | **speedup**: ~7.69% faster   ---   **Scan parquet**  **size**: ~78.42% smaller  **Rows:** ~71.08% fewer rows |

**Explanation:**

- **Total Runtime:** Partitioned table queries are slightly faster (3.6s vs. 3.9s) due to more efficient data access.
- **Scan Parquet:** This metric reflects the efficiency of how Spark reads data from Parquet files, crucial for performance analysis
- **Size of Files Read:** Partitioning significantly reduces the size of the data read (308.0 MiB vs. 66.4 MiB), demonstrating how partitioning allows Spark to target only relevant data.
- **Number of Output Rows:** Partitioned tables return fewer rows (13,290,528 vs. 3,844,009) focusing only on the required partitions.

**Conclusion:** Partitioning enhances performance by minimizing data reads and focusing on specific partitions, making it highly beneficial for large datasets with structured partitioning.

---

## 2. Use compression

Compression significantly speeds up query performance by reducing the data size, which in turn decreases storage space requirements. It also reduces the amount of data that needs to be scanned, minimizing query overhead.

Benefits**:** Compression is particularly useful when storage space is limited or when you need to reduce the size of files containing large amounts of data.

Compression can be achieved in various ways, depending on the type of data and the tools being used. Here are some common methods that can be applied.

**Data Compression in Blendata:**

- **Parquet :** These are file formats designed for storing compressed data in a columnar format, making them well-suited for large-scale data analytics, particularly in systems that use Hadoop, Spark, and Hive.

**Lossless Compression**: This method compresses data without any loss. When decompressed, the data will be identical to the original.

- **Snappy**: Designed for speed, it's often used in systems that require fast data processing.
- **GZIP**: Commonly used in Unix/Linux systems, it can compress various file types like Parquet, CSV, JSON, and XML.

**Blendata: Service Management Menu in the “Compact Data (Hot-Warm)”**

**Compact Data (Hot-Warm)**: This process involves creating a service to manage data efficiently in alignment with the system's storage resources, enhancing overall performance. The approach compresses data files to reduce their size, particularly for assets like time-series data, with partitions selected based solely on timestamps.

**Steps to Create a Compact Data (Hot-Warm) Service**

1. Initial Setup: Configure the service by assigning a name in the File Type Service Name field. Set up notifications in the Job Triggering section, add tags to the service, and then click Create to finalize the service creation.

![](https://blendata.atlassian.net/wiki/download/attachments/2202731347/AD_4nXccy139vHqLsCDyhndKjYyf2fBDLczA93VHL1nopeNExkhQlTtckn4yxU1RIXXV9sf-_aq13kjo0e3zW8Qll5DblVYQ_lfJOn0MSF2kHGmXeeSGr4XYYWX1KwBaAjTBKCt75jhWKl_GABUwZzEpFuip4TU%3Fkey=hfxOw1K9Db4wqcNNMo9HFA?api=v2)

2. Step 2: Enter the configuration settings for the service in the Service Parameters section. The system will provide a basic template for users to fill in.

![](https://blendata.atlassian.net/wiki/download/attachments/2202731347/AD_4nXe67KRwNkV52S_96Q5qCjM6XnTV2zv1r-FRKmWJ1Kkc0pnd0l44XR8YvzTxvXuIko-XORt37z744ghZ7EhRL89o4Y2aaJ9loCrwxKbRDFiRoAncsrmqDRn9Gfin8K1wWShtB9vpOjQFYTvqKfW3k8yE6EOW%3Fkey=hfxOw1K9Db4wqcNNMo9HFA?api=v2)

---

## 3. Optimize file sizes

Managing file size by optimizing it is a crucial factor in enhancing the efficiency of data retrieval and processing, including:

1. Improved Query Performance: Smaller data sizes reduce scan times.
2. Reduced Query Cost.

**Blendata: Service Management Menu in the “Job Merge”**

**Job Merge:** This process merges table files in storage to reduce the number of files, resulting in larger file sizes. It is useful when a table contains a large number of files, and you want to reduce this number to speed up data reading or writing. With fewer files, the system requires fewer file operations, making data management easier and improving query performance.

**Steps to Create a Job Merge Service:**

1. Initial Setup: Configure the service by assigning a name in the *File Type Service Name* field. Set up notifications in the *Job Triggering* section, add tags to the service, and then click *Create* to finalize the service creation.

![](https://blendata.atlassian.net/wiki/download/attachments/2202731347/AD_4nXfSyatEC6Yuq7FSu8vytxLceZY2MA44zpAzWcO5xv64GzueoEvL6WpqECzCCzpvjlan3Saib0tqdU0U8SZ6v23GSspkt_ERuuYSTuOYv8bmXjxq7Hd4Ck7BQzVCQ4qF659kYTWRMpgGunaWiqdwGYNSc6So%3Fkey=hfxOw1K9Db4wqcNNMo9HFA?api=v2)

2. Enter the configuration settings for the service in the Service Parameters section, where the system provides a basic template for users to specify tableNames and realmName.

![](https://blendata.atlassian.net/wiki/download/attachments/2202731347/AD_4nXelMZ7J5g1DWCzUGpKq-HGVuHHfemLDSX6zQRdMOGIICMnK5-y6Vg8C6OYFHJfWObkAIFTxhMH0jWgBI-4Hn4NcXeQtY1z9cwVo3ZUSGFo4OKTo76Ar9cxA1FQGQNo4MKgU9Yvwvmv2VikZTfVLwcBH8BwB%3Fkey=hfxOw1K9Db4wqcNNMo9HFA?api=v2)

**Choosing Between Merging Files or Compressing Files:**

- Choose “**Job Merge Service**” when you want to improve the efficiency of file management and data read/write operations in a system with a large number of files. Merging files helps reduce the overhead of opening and closing multiple files.
- Choose “**Compact Data (Hot-Warm) Service**” when you want to reduce file size to save storage space or to speed up data transfer over the network.

---

## 4. Use columnar file formats

Using Columnar File Formats is Formats like **Apache Parquet** are well-suited for large-scale data analytics. Columnar file formats store data by columns instead of rows, allowing for more efficient compression and data management.

Columnar file formats can employ various data compression strategies, such as:

- **Run-Length Encoding**: Used to compress columns with many repeated values by storing the value once along with the count of repetitions.
- **Dictionary Encoding**: Replaces each value with a pointer to a lookup table.
- **Text Compression**: Uses standard compression formats like gzip, Snappy, and zstd.

Block Size:

- **Parquet**: The default block size is 128 MB.

Time-Based Data Management:

- Partitioning

---

## Query tuning

| Field | Value |
|---|---|
| **URL** | https://blendata.atlassian.net/wiki/spaces/bde460/pages/2202731414/Query+tuning |
| **Last modified** | 2025-01-17T09:21:59.441Z |
| **Breadcrumb** | Blendata Enterprise V.4.6.0 > General References > Tuning & optimization |

Latest Update: September 6, 2024

## 1. Using ORDER BY and LIMIT

1. Using  LIMIT clause affects runtime by controlling the volume of data sent. A large result set can take longer to transmit to the client due to increased network traffic. Therefore, applying LIMIT can enhance query performance if you don't need to view all data.
2. Using ORDER BY increases processing time in Spark, as it requires sorting all data, which is resource-intensive. However, if you need to display results in a specific order or retrieve the top or bottom N values, use LIMIT to reduce sorting costs and speed up query execution.

For example, the following table summarizes the runtime for a dataset with approximately 88,741 KB. Data source: [*Kaggle*](https://www.kaggle.com/datasets/zusmani/pakistans-largest-ecommerce-dataset?resource=download)

This table is used to compare the results of using ORDER BY versus not using ORDER BY.

|  |  |
| --- | --- |
| **Query** | **Query time** |
| SELECT \* FROM e\_commerce  ORDER BY item\_id | 9.2 seconds |
| SELECT \* FROM e\_commerce | 1.9 seconds |
| The absence of ORDER BY helps to speed up | ≈79.35% faster |

This table compares the performance between using ORDER BY alone and using ORDER BY in conjunction with LIMIT.

|  |  |
| --- | --- |
| **Query** | **Query time** |
| SELECT \* FROM e\_commerce  ORDER BY item\_id  *adjust the record limit on Blendata to 100,000 to assume no limit in large data sets.*  *Note: Normally, Blendata automatically sets the record limit to 2,000* | 55.8 seconds |
| SELECT \* FROM e\_commerce  ORDER BY item\_id  LIMIT 2000 | 10.1 seconds |
| Using ORDER BY with LIMIT can improve performance | ≈81.86% |

---

## 2. Optimizing Joins

**2.1 Choosing Join Order:** Place the larger table on the left (left join) and the smaller table on the right to enhance processing efficiency.

- **Build a Lookup Table:** Create a lookup table from the smaller table (on the right) and distribute it to worker nodes.
- **Stream the Larger Table:** The larger table (on the left) will be streamed and rows joined by matching values found in the lookup table.

**Advantage**: Using a lookup table created from the smaller table and distributed to worker nodes makes the join operation more efficient.

For example, the following table summarizes the runtime for a dataset with approximately 88,741 KB. Data source: [*Kaggle*](https://www.kaggle.com/datasets/zusmani/pakistans-largest-ecommerce-dataset?resource=download)

|  |  |
| --- | --- |
| **Query** | **Query time** |
| SELECT      ec.item\_id,  ec.category\_name\_1,      ec.price,ec.qty\_ordered,      ec.grand\_total,d.top\_discount,      ec.price - d.top\_discount AS net\_price  FROM   e\_commerce ec  LEFT JOIN      top\_discount d ON      d.category\_product = ec.category\_name\_1    *Note: LEFT JOIN with the smaller table on the right side of the join condition.* | 2.5 seconds |
| SELECT      ec.item\_id,  ec.category\_name\_1,      ec.price,  ec.qty\_ordered,      ec.grand\_total, d.top\_discount,      ec.price - d.top\_discount AS net\_price  FROM    e\_commerce ec  RIGHT JOIN      top\_discount d ON      d.category\_product = ec.category\_name\_1    *Note: RIGHT JOIN with the smaller table on the right side of the join condition.* | 10.4 seconds |
| Using a large table on the left (LEFT JOIN) speeds up | ~76.0 % faster |

**2.2 Joining Partitioned Tables:** If the table is in a time-series format, the Blendata platform will handle it.

When querying partitioned tables, ensure you apply filters to all partition keys for each table being queried. This practice helps minimize the number of file reads. For example, the table below summarizes the runtime for datasets of approximately 16 KB and 23 KB.

|  |  |
| --- | --- |
| **Query** | **Query time** |
| SELECT AVG(l\_extendedprice)  FROM line\_item JOIN orders *ON*  (l\_orderkey = o\_orderkey)  *AND* o\_orderdate = '1993-07-08' | 4.6  seconds |
| SELECT AVG(l\_extendedprice)  FROM line\_item JOIN orders *ON*   (l\_orderkey = o\_orderkey  *AND* l\_orderdate = o\_orderdate)  *AND* o\_orderdate = '1993-07-08' | 3.1 seconds |
| Speeds up | ~32.61 % faster |

**The Differences Are:**

- Statement 1 may include data with different dates in line\_item but matching the date filtered in orders.
- Statement 2 will include only rows where the dates in both line\_item and orders match and also requires filtering by o\_orderdate (providing clearer and more specific results).

---

## 3. Use approximate functions

When analyzing large datasets, COUNT(DISTINCT column) provides 100% accurate results because it directly counts unique values. However, if exact numbers are not necessary, consider using approx\_count\_distinct(column), an approximation technique that estimates the number of unique values without storing or processing all the data. The difference is that approx\_count\_distinct(column) uses fewer resources and is faster compared to COUNT(DISTINCT column), though it offers reasonably high accuracy, but not 100%.

For example, the following table summarizes the runtime for a dataset with approximately 88,741 KB. Data source: [*Kaggle*](https://www.kaggle.com/datasets/zusmani/pakistans-largest-ecommerce-dataset?resource=download)

|  |  |
| --- | --- |
| **Query** | **Query time** |
| SELECT COUNT(DISTINCT item\_id) AS count\_distinct  FROM e\_commerce | 13.2 seconds |
| SELECT approx\_count\_distinct(item\_id) AS approx\_distinct\_count  FROM e\_commerce | 9.1 seconds |
| Speedup | ~ 31.06% faster |

For example, the following table summarizes the runtime for a dataset with approximately 5,314 KB

|  |  |
| --- | --- |
| **Query** | **Query time** |
| SELECT PERCENTILE\_CONT (0.33) WITHIN GROUP (ORDER BY duration\_seconds) AS percentile33  FROM job\_history | 24.2 seconds |
| SELECT percentile\_approx(duration\_seconds, 0.33) AS percentile33  FROM job\_history | 2.7 seconds |
| Speedup | ~ 88.8% faster |

---

## 4. Only include the columns that you need

selecting only the necessary columns, limiting the use of SELECT instead of using SELECT \*

For example, the following table summarizes the runtime for a dataset with approximately 88,741 KB

|  |  |
| --- | --- |
| **Query** | **Query time** |
| SELECT \* FROM e\_commerce  WHERE category\_name\_1 = 'Soghaat'    AND status = 'canceled' | 4.6 seconds |
| SELECT customer\_id, category\_name\_1, status  FROM e\_commerce  WHERE category\_name\_1 = 'Soghaat'    AND status = 'canceled' | 2.3 seconds |
| Speedup | ~41.67% faster |

---

- <https://blendata.atlassian.net/wiki/spaces/bde460/pages/2210627604/Airflow+Agent+Configuration?atlOrigin=eyJpIjoiM2FhZTU5ZGViMGEwNDQyYTk3ZTYyMTJmNGIzNTE5Y2MiLCJwIjoiYyJ9>

## Cerberus Configuration

| Field | Value |
|---|---|
| **URL** | https://blendata.atlassian.net/wiki/spaces/bde460/pages/2202731458/Cerberus+Configuration |
| **Last modified** | 2025-01-17T09:21:59.856Z |
| **Breadcrumb** | Blendata Enterprise V.4.6.0 > Administrator User Manual > Post-Installation > Configuration |

[data-colorid=ley26ug0gj]{color:#97a0af} html[data-color-mode=dark] [data-colorid=ley26ug0gj]{color:#505968}Configuration files are located in the folder `config`. This page describes the details of each configuration property in the following files.

- [application.properties](#cerberus-application-properties)
- [service.properties](#cerberus-service-properties)
- [email.properties](#cerberus-email-properties)

### application.properties

| **Property** | **Default** | **Description** |
| --- | --- | --- |
| gtemplar.application.group | Blendata | Application group information. |
| gtemplar.application.id | 1 | Application ID Information. |
| gtemplar.application.name | CERBERUS | Application name information. |
| gtemplar.application.version | 3.3.0 | Application version information. |
| gtemplar.application.description | readme.md | Application description information, in Markdown file format. |
| gtemplar.application.copyright | COPYRIGHT 2020 G-ABLE CO., LTD. | Application copyright information. |
|  |  |  |
| management.endpoint.shutdown.enabled | true | Whether to enable the shutdown endpoint [[1]](#cerberus-config-ref). |
| management.endpoints.web.exposure.include | \* | Select ID of monitoring and managing endpoints to expose [[1]](#cerberus-config-ref). By Default, setting to \* means expose all endpoints. |
|  |  |  |
| root.home.path | ${CERBERUS\_HOME} | The location of Cerberus home directory. Default value is system environment `CERBERUS_HOME`. |
| logging.config | ${root.home.path}/config/logback.xml | The location of logging config file [[2]](#cerberus-config-ref). |
| server.port | 11020 | Cerberus server HTTP/HTTPS port [[3]](#cerberus-config-ref). |
| server.servlet.context-path | /cerberus/v1 | Cerberus root context path [[4]](#cerberus-config-ref). By default, application can be accessed from https://localhost:11020/cerberus/v1. |
| server.ssl.key-store | ${root.home.path}/ssl/server.keystore.jks | Path to the key store that holds the SSL certificate to enable HTTPS protocol [[4]](#cerberus-config-ref). You should generate a new certificate associated with public IP or domain name. |
| server.ssl.key-store-password |  | Password used to access the key in the key store [[4]](#cerberus-config-ref). |
| server.ssl.key-password |  | Password used to access the key store [[4]](#cerberus-config-ref). |
| spring.main.banner-mode | off | Mode used to display the banner when the application runs [4]. By default, disable Spring banner printed on console at startup. |
|  |  |  |
| database.master | cerberus | Cerberus database name. |
| spring.datasource.url | jdbc:mysql://localhost:13306/${database.master}?useUnicode=true | Database connection in JDBC URL format [[4]](#cerberus-config-ref). |
| spring.datasource.username | cerberus | Database login username [[4]](#cerberus-config-ref). |
| spring.datasource.password |  | Database login password [[4]](#cerberus-config-ref). |
| spring.datasource.driver-class | com.mysql.jdbc.Driver | JDBC driver class name to connect to application database [[4]](#cerberus-config-ref). |
| spring.datasource.platform | mysql | Specific database script platform [[4]](#cerberus-config-ref) i.e. schema-mysql.sql, data-mysql.sql. |
| spring.datasource.initialization-mode | always | Mode to initialize the database with the embedded DDL script in Cerberus application [[5]](#cerberus-config-ref).   - `always`: to initialize the database. Cerberus will create all required tables in database cerberus as the application startup. - `never`: to skip database initialization as the application startup.   \* Set to `always` on the first time installing the application. |
| spring.datasource.continue-on-error | true | Whether to stop if an error occurs while initializing the database [[5]](#cerberus-config-ref). |
|  |  |  |
| logging.level.com.gable.templar.cerberus.filter.CustomRequestLoggingFilter | DEBUG | Enable logging incoming request. Comment out this property to disable logging incoming request [[6]](#cerberus-config-ref). |
| filter.payload.enable | true | Whether to print payload in the incoming request log message [[6]](#cerberus-config-ref). |
| filter.payload.maxLength | 10000 | Maximum length of the payload to be included in the incoming request log message [[6]](#cerberus-config-ref). |
| jasypt.encryptor.bean | customJasyptEncryptor | Specific custom encryptor class to use for encrypting password properties in file conguration `application.properties`. |
| cache.user.clear.fixed.rate | 60000 | Time interval to clear user cache information. |
| spring.ldap.authen.read-attributes | false | Integrating with LDAP authentication, whether to require LDAP users to have read permissions on their own attributes. |

### service.properties

| **Property** | **Default** | **Description** |
| --- | --- | --- |
| service.notification.url | <https://localhost:10008/noti> | Notification service url and context path. |

- The notification endpoints provided for each service are specified by the properties started with prefix `notification`. These properties are no need to configure.

### email.properties

| **Property** | **Default** | **Description** |
| --- | --- | --- |
| mail.sender.name | Blendata Administrator | E-mail sender name. |
| mail.sender.email | admin@blendata.co | E-mail sender e-mail address. |
| mail.newaccount.subject | New User Account | The subject of e-mail to notify new user account. |
| mail.newaccount.message | <h4>Dear Valued Customer</h4> <p>Your username is activated. You can login for use blendata as below link</p> $PAGE\_URL <br/><br/> \Username : <span style='color:blue'>$USERNAME</span> <br/> \Password : <span style='color:blue'>$PASSWORD</span> <br/><br/><br/> <h4>Yours sincerely,</h4> \G-ABLE Company Limited <br/><br/><br/> <span style='color:red'>Please keep this email confidential.</span> <br/> | The template of e-mail message to notify new user account in HTML format. |
| mail.resetpwd.subject | Your New Login Password | The subject of e-mail to notify new password. |
| mail.resetpwd.message | <h4>Dear Valued Customer</h4> \ <p>With reference to your request to receive the new login password, we would like to inform you that the Login Password has been initially set as follows:</p> \ <br/> \ Username: <span style='color:blue'>$USERNAME</span> \ <br/> \ New Login Password: <span style='color:blue'>$PASSWORD</span> \ <br/><br/> \ You can login for use blendata as below link: \ <br/> \ $PAGE\_URL \ <br/><br/><br/> \ <h4>Yours sincerely,</h4> \ G-ABLE Company Limited \ <br/><br/><br/> \ <span style='color:red'>Email Disclaimer:</span> \ This message contains confidential information and is intended only for the individual named. If you are not the named addressee you should not disseminate, distribute or copy this e-mail. Please notify the sender immediately by e-mail if you have received this e-mail by mistake and delete this e-mail from your system. E-mail transmission cannot be guaranteed to be secure or error-free as information could be intercepted, corrupted, lost, destroyed, arrive late or incomplete, or contain viruses. The sender therefore does not accept liability for any errors or omissions in the contents of this message, which arise as a result of e-mail transmission. \ <br/> | The template of e-mail message to notify new password in HTML format. |

## References

1. Spring Boot Actuator: Production-ready Features. <https://docs.spring.io/spring-boot/docs/current/reference/html/production-ready-features.html>.
2. Logging. [https://docs.spring.io/spring-boot/docs/1.5.x/reference/html/howto-logging.html.](https://docs.spring.io/spring-boot/docs/1.5.x/reference/html/howto-logging.html).
3. Embedded Web Servers. <https://docs.spring.io/spring-boot/docs/2.0.x/reference/html/howto-embedded-web-servers.html>.
4. Appendix A. Common application properties. <https://docs.spring.io/spring-boot/docs/2.0.5.RELEASE/reference/html/common-application-properties.html>.
5. Database Initialization. <https://docs.spring.io/spring-boot/docs/2.0.5.RELEASE/reference/html/howto-database-initialization.html>.
6. Class AbstractRequestLoggingFilter. <https://docs.spring.io/spring-framework/docs/5.1.6.RELEASE_to_5.2.0.M1/Spring%20Framework%205.2.0.M1/org/springframework/web/filter/AbstractRequestLoggingFilter.html>.

## Hera Configuration

| Field | Value |
|---|---|
| **URL** | https://blendata.atlassian.net/wiki/spaces/bde460/pages/2202731492/Hera+Configuration |
| **Last modified** | 2025-01-17T09:22:00.371Z |
| **Breadcrumb** | Blendata Enterprise V.4.6.0 > Administrator User Manual > Post-Installation > Configuration |

Configuration files are located in the folder `config`. This page describes the details of each configuration property in the following files.

- [application.properties](#hera-application-properties)
- [service.properties](#hera-service-properties)
- [quartz.properties](#hera-quartz-properties)
- [database-retention-config.json](#hera-db-retention-properties)
- [web.conf](#hera-web-conf) (Linux symbolic link to file `../web/assets/config/config.dev.json`)

### application.properties

| **Property** | **Default** | **Description** |
| --- | --- | --- |
| gtemplar.application.group | Blendata | Application group information. |
| gtemplar.application.id | 1 | Application ID Information. |
| gtemplar.application.name | HERA | Application name information. |
| gtemplar.application.secret |  |  |
| gtemplar.application.version | 3.3.0 | Application version information. |
| gtemplar.application.description | readme.md | Application description information, in Markdown file format. |
| gtemplar.application.copyright | COPYRIGHT 2020 G-ABLE CO., LTD. | Application copyright information. |
| gtemplar.application.url | https://localhost:11010 | Hera service url. Job scheduler management uses this property for a job that calls Hera web service. |
|  |  |  |
| root.home.path | ${HERA\_HOME} | The location of Hera home directory. Default value is system environment `HERA_HOME`. |
| root.data.path | /data/hera | The location of Hera data directory. |
| logging.config | ${root.home.path}/config/logback.xml | The location of Hera logging config file [[2]](#hera-ref). |
|  |  |  |
| management.endpoint.shutdown.enabled | true | Whether to enable the shutdown endpoint [[1]](#hera-ref). |
| management.endpoints.web.exposure.include | \* | Select ID of monitoring and managing endpoints to expose [[1]](#hera-ref). By Default, setting to \* means expose all endpoints. |
|  |  |  |
| server.port | 11010 | Hera server HTTPS port [[2]](#hera-ref). |
| server.ssl.key-store | ${root.home.path}/ssl/server.keystore.jks | Path to the key store that holds the SSL certificate to enable HTTPS protocol [[2]](#hera-ref). You should generate a new certificate associated with public IP or domain name. |
| server.ssl.key-store-password |  | Password used to access the key in the key store [[2]](#hera-ref). |
| server.ssl.key-password |  | Password used to access the key store [[2]](#hera-ref). |
|  |  |  |
| spring.datasource.url | jdbc:mysql://localhost:3306/hera?characterEncoding=utf8&useUnicode=true&useJDBCCompliantTimezoneShift=true&useLegacyDatetimeCode=false&serverTimezone=UTC&useSSL=false | Database connection in JDBC URL format [[2]](#hera-ref). |
| spring.datasource.jdbc-url | ${spring.datasource.url} | Database connection in JDBC URL format [[2]](#hera-ref). |
| spring.datasource.username | hera | Database login username [[2]](#hera-ref). |
| spring.datasource.password |  | Database login password [[2]](#hera-ref). |
| spring.datasource.driver-class | com.mysql.jdbc.Driver | JDBC driver class name to connect to application database [[2]](#hera-ref). |
| spring.datasource.platform | mysql | Specific database script platform [[2]](#hera-ref) i.e. schema-mysql.sql. |
| spring.datasource.leak-detection-threshold | 60000 | HikariCP leak detection threshold. The timeout for the connection to be occupied before a message is logged indicating the connection leaked [[3]](#hera-ref). |
| spring.datasource.maximum-pool-size | 10 | HikariCP maximum database connection pool size [[3]](#hera-ref). |
| spring.datasource.connection-timeout | 30000 | HikariCP database connection timeout which is the maximum time to wait for the database connection [[3]](#hera-ref). |
| spring.jpa.properties.hibernate.dialect | org.hibernate.dialect.MySQL5InnoDBDialect | Optimize Hibernate to generate SQL for the specific database [[2]](#hera-ref). |
| spring.jpa.hibernate.ddl-auto | update | Hibernate DDL mode (create, create-drop, validate, update). Default to `update` which allows Hibernate to auto update tables schema [[2]](#hera-ref). |
| spring.jpa.open-in-view | false | Register OpenEntityManagerInViewInterceptor. Binds a JPA EntityManager to the thread for the entire processing of the request. Disable by default. Enable creates the database connection since web request until the ending of that request which is not recommended for database production performance [[2]](#hera-ref). |
|  |  |  |
| spring.quartz.job-store-type | jdbc | Quartz job store type [[2]](#hera-ref). As Hera store job configuration by database, thus setting this property value to `jdbc`. |
| spring.quartz.jdbc.initialize-schema | never | Mode to initialize QUARTZ database with the embedded DDL script in Hera application [[2]](#hera-ref).   - `always`: to initialize QUARTZ database tables by executing DDL script on application startup. - `never`: to skip database initialization on the application startup.   \* Set to `always` on the first time installing the application [[2]](#hera-ref). |
| spring.quartz.jdbc.comment-prefix | # | Prefixes for single-line comments in SQL initialization scripts [[2]](#hera-ref). |
| spring.quartz.jdbc.schema | classpath:/tables\_mysql\_innodb.sql | Path to the SQL file to use to initialize the database schema [[2]](#hera-ref). |
|  |  |  |
| rsa.file.path | ${root.home.path}/certs/keystore.jks | Path to the key store used for datasource options encryption. |
| rsa.alias.name | mykey | An alias name to get key entry from file key store specified by property `rsa.file.path`. |
| rsa.password |  | Password used to access the key store specified by property `rsa.file.path`. |
|  |  |  |
| spring.main.banner-mode | off | Mode used to display the banner when the application runs [[2]](#hera-ref). By default, disable Spring banner printed on console at startup. |
| spring.mvc.async.request-timeout | 3600000 | Amount of time before asynchronous request handling times out [[2]](#hera-ref). |
| spring.mvc.async.timeout | 3600000 | Read timeout when reading from Input stream when a connection is established to a resource [[4]](#hera-ref). |
| spring.servlet.multipart.max-file-size | 209715200 | Maximum file size [[2]](#hera-ref). By default, upload file size cannot exceed 200MB. |
| spring.servlet.multipart.max-request-size | 209715200 | Maximum request size [[2]](#hera-ref). By default, `multipart/form-data` request size cannot exceed 200MB. |
| spring.devtools.restart.exclude | static/\*\* | Patterns that should be excluded from triggering a full restart [[2]](#hera-ref). |
|  |  |  |
| http.connect.timeout | 43200000 | Connection timeout (milliseconds) on REST API service calling [[5]](#hera-ref). |
| http.read.timeout | 43200000 | Socket timeout (milliseconds) on REST API service calling [[5]](#hera-ref). |
| job.service.http.connect.timeout | 86400000 | Connection timeout (milliseconds) on REST API service calling to Hera module. |
| job.service.http.read.timeout | 86400000 | Socket timeout (milliseconds) on REST API service calling to Hera module. |
|  |  |  |
| hera.calf.health.interval | 30 | Time interval (seconds) to check Brow Cow status. |
|  |  |  |
| data.state.hot.compression | snappy | The default state table compression file format for the data store in Hot state (uncompressed,snappy,gzip). |
| data.state.warm.compression | snappy | The default state table compression file format for the data store in Warm state (uncompressed,snappy,gzip). |
| data.state.cold.compression | gzip | The default state table compression file format for the data store in Cold state (uncompressed,snappy,gzip). |
| data.compact.intercept.stream | false | Whether to stop stream before processing compaction or not. |
|  |  |  |
| file.upload | ${root.data.path}/upload | Path to the directory of upload files including images and temporary files. |
| image.upload.location | ${file.upload}/image | Path to the directory of image files i.e. user profile's image, and images of projects, applications, dashboards. |
|  |  |  |
| noti.query.limit | 100000 | Default maximum records of notification query results. |
| noti.email.temp\_path | ${root.data.path}/noti/tmp | Path to the directory of temporary files on sending an e-mail with file attachments. |
|  |  |  |
| history.query-by-user.save-num | 100 | Maximum records to save each users' query history. |
|  |  |  |
| landing.page.file.path | ${root.data.path}/landing-page/realm/ | Path to the directory of landing pages saved state i.e. recently viewed projects, applications. dashboards, and the favorite list of projects, applications, dashboards. |
|  |  |  |
| quartz.config.path | ${root.home.path}/config | Path to the directory of configuration file `quartz.properties`. |
| database.retention.config.path | ${root.home.path}/config | Path to the directory of configuration file `database-retention-config.json`. |
| spring.custom.service.path | ${root.home.path}/jars | Path to the directory of Hera custom services jar files. |
|  |  |  |
| user.import.template\_file | ${root.home.path}/config/user\_import\_template.xlsx | Path to file template for importing users. |
|  |  |  |
| kafka.preview.metadata.poll.time | 1000 | The blocking time to wait for consuming records from Kafka [[6]](#hera-ref). |
| kafka.preview.poll.time | 1000 | The blocking time to wait for consuming records from Kafka [[6]](#hera-ref). |
|  |  |  |
| logging.level.com.gable.templar.hera.filter.CustomRequestLoggingFilter | DEBUG | Enable logging incoming request. Comment out this property to disable logging incoming request [[7]](#hera-ref). |
| filter.payload.enable | true | Whether to print payload in the incoming request log message [[7]](#hera-ref). |
| filter.payload.maxLength | 50000 | Maximum length of the payload to be included in the incoming request log message [[7]](#hera-ref). |
|  |  |  |
| jasypt.encryptor.bean | customJasyptEncryptor | Specific custom encryptor class to use for encrypting password properties in file conguration `application.properties`. |
|  |  |  |
| # saml.properties.filepath | ${root.home.path}/config/onelogin.saml.properties | Path to SAML configuration file. Uncomment this properties to enable SAML authentication integration. (see more details about SAML integration in [Blendata - SAML SSO Integration](https://blendata.atlassian.net/wiki/spaces/KB/pages/475398145/Blendata+-+SAML+SSO+Integration)). |

### service.properties

| **Property** | **Default** | **Description** |
| --- | --- | --- |
| service.cerberus.url | <https://localhost:11020/cerberus/v1> | Cerberus service url and context path. |
| service.notification.url | <https://localhost:10008/noti> | Notification service url and context path. |
| service.cerberus.client.id |  | The application ID registered in authorization server (Cerberus). This default value cannot be changed. |
| service.cerberus.client.secret |  | The application secret registered in authorization server (Cerberus). This default value cannot be changed. |

- Cerberus endpoints provided for each service are specified by the properties started with prefix `cerberus` and `service.cerberus`. These properties are no need to configure.
- Notification endpoints provided for each service are specified by the properties started with prefix `notification`. These properties are no need to configure.
- Zeus endpoints provided for each service are specified by the properties started with prefix `zeus`. These properties are no need to configure.
- Brown Cow endpoints provided for each service are specified by the properties started with prefix `hera.calf`. These properties are no need to configure.

### quartz.properties

| **Property** | **Default** | **Description** |
| --- | --- | --- |
| org.quartz.threadPool.class | org.quartz.simpl.SimpleThreadPool | The name of the ThreadPool implementation [[8]](#hera-ref). |
| org.quartz.threadPool.threadCount | 20 | The number of threads that are available for concurrent execution of jobs [[8]](#hera-ref). |
| org.quartz.threadPool.threadsInheritContextClassLoaderOfInitializingThread | TRUE | Whether new threads to inherit the Classloader of the parent creating thread or not [[8]](#hera-ref). |
|  |  |  |
| org.quartz.jobStore.class | org.quartz.impl.jdbcjobstore.JobStoreTX | Quartz job storage machanishm. Setting to `JobStoreTX` to store job data on the database [[9]](#hera-ref). |
| org.quartz.jobStore.driverDelegateClass | org.quartz.impl.jdbcjobstore.StdJDBCDelegate | The particular ‘dialects’ of varies database systems [[9]](#hera-ref). |
| org.quartz.jobStore.dataSource | quartzDataSource | The name of one the DataSources defined in the configuration properties file. See the [configuration docs for DataSources](http://www.quartz-scheduler.org/documentation/quartz-2.3.0/configuration/ConfigDataSources.html) for more information [[9]](#hera-ref). |

### database-retention-config.json

| **Property** | **Default** | **Description** |
| --- | --- | --- |
| tableName |  | Database table name to delete records, for example, `job_history`. |
| dateColumnName |  | Database table column name which has type `datetime` and is a time to compare for retention, for example, `created_date`. |
| olderThan |  | The number of previous days to retention, for example, `30`. |
| columnId |  | Database table column name which is primary key, for example `id`. |

### web.conf

| **Property** | **Default** | **Description** |
| --- | --- | --- |
| productName | Blendata | Product Name. |
| productVersion | v3.3.0 | Product Version. |
| productContact | <https://blendata.co/contact-us/> | Contact Information. |
| copyrightNotice | Copyright® 2019. G-Able Company Limited. All right reserved. | Copyright notification. |
| [apiServer](#web-apiServer) |  | Web service setting webpage calling to. |
| uploadLimit | 209715200 | Maximum file size to upload. By default, upload file size cannot exceed 200MB. |
| [cookie](#web-cookie) |  | Cookie configuration. |
| [securityPolicy](#web-securityPolicy) |  | Security configuration. |
| [devMode](#web-devMode) |  | Advance configuration. |
| [SAMLConfig](#web-SAMLConfig) |  | SAML configuration. |
| defaultDatePicker | 1h | Default date time range setting on Data exploration page. |
| [refreshDataInterval](#web-refreshDataInterval) |  | Time interval configuration to reload data on Service management page. |

- apiServer

| **Property** | **Default** | **Description** |
| --- | --- | --- |
| hera | <https://localhost:11010> | Hera service url. The destination url must be reachable from the client machine. |

- cookie

| **Property** | **Default** | **Description** |
| --- | --- | --- |
| secure | true | Setting flag to `true`, the cookie is sent to server using a secure connection (SSL/HTTPS) only [[10]](#hera-ref). |
| sameSite | Strict | Cookie `sameSite` options (None, Lax, Strict). Default to`Strict`, the cookie is sent only to the same site as the one that originated it [[10]](#hera-ref). |

- securityPolicy

| **Property** | **Default** | **Description** |
| --- | --- | --- |
| userIdle.idle | 900 | Maximum time (seconds) of user inactivity before the system force user logout. |
| userIdle.ping | 120 | Time interval (seconds) to monitor user activity, have to less than `userIdle.idle` setting. |
| noUserExpiryDate | 60 | Default expiry days setting since user created date. |

- devMode

| **Property** | **Default** | **Description** |
| --- | --- | --- |
| showEditStreamService | false | Whether to show Stream management menu **‘Edit Stream’** or not. |
| showCreateTableSourceType | false | Whether to show Create table options **'Source Type'** or not. |
| showUpdateUserSourceMenu | false | Whether to show User management menu **'Update user source'** to change user authentication source or not. |
| showUpdateLicenseCpuMenu | false | Whether to show License management menu **'Update Cpu'** to edit registered Zeus CPU or not. |

- SAMLConfig

| **Property** | **Default** | **Description** |
| --- | --- | --- |
| showSAMLLoginButton | false | Whether to show SAML login button on Login page or not. |

- refreshDataInterval

| **Property** | **Default** | **Description** |
| --- | --- | --- |
| service-job | 20000 | Time interval (milliseconds) to reload data on Service management submenus i.e. Data management, Data processing, Extension Service. |
| service-stream | 20000 | Time interval (milliseconds) to reload data on Service management submenus i.e. Stream service. |
| service-transform | 10000 | Time interval (milliseconds) to reload data on Service management submenus i.e. Data transformation. |

## References

1. Spring Boot Actuator: Production-ready Features. <https://docs.spring.io/spring-boot/docs/current/reference/html/production-ready-features.html>.
2. Common Application properties. <https://docs.spring.io/spring-boot/docs/2.2.5.RELEASE/reference/html/appendix-application-properties.html#common-application-properties>.
3. HikariCP. <https://github.com/brettwooldridge/HikariCP> .
4. Class URLConnection. <https://docs.oracle.com/javase/8/docs/api/java/net/URLConnection.html>.
5. Class RequestConfig. <https://www.javadoc.io/doc/org.apache.httpcomponents/httpclient/4.5.5/org/apache/http/client/config/RequestConfig.html>.
6. Class KafkaConsumer<K,V>. <https://kafka.apache.org/20/javadoc/org/apache/kafka/clients/consumer/KafkaConsumer.html#poll-java.time.Duration->.
7. Class AbstractRequestLoggingFilter. <https://docs.spring.io/spring-framework/docs/5.1.6.RELEASE_to_5.2.0.M1/Spring%20Framework%205.2.0.M1/org/springframework/web/filter/AbstractRequestLoggingFilter.html>.
8. Configure ThreadPool Settings. <http://www.quartz-scheduler.org/documentation/quartz-2.3.0/configuration/ConfigThreadPool.html>.
9. Configure JDBC-JobStoreTX. <http://www.quartz-scheduler.org/documentation/quartz-2.3.0/configuration/ConfigJobStoreTX.html>.
10. Using HTTP cookies. <https://developer.mozilla.org/en-US/docs/Web/HTTP/Cookies>.

## Zeus Configuration

| Field | Value |
|---|---|
| **URL** | https://blendata.atlassian.net/wiki/spaces/bde460/pages/2202731539/Zeus+Configuration |
| **Last modified** | 2025-01-17T09:22:00.545Z |
| **Breadcrumb** | Blendata Enterprise V.4.6.0 > Administrator User Manual > Post-Installation > Configuration |

Configuration files are located in the folder `config`. This page describes the details of each configuration property in the following files.

- [application.properties](#zeus-application-properties)
- [service.properties](#zeus-service-properties)

For Spark configuration files

- spark.conf: see more information on [https://spark.apache.org/docs/2.4.7/configuration.html](https://spark.apache.org/docs/2.4.5/configuration.html).
- hive-site.xml (Linux symbolic link to file `../spark/conf/hive-site.xml`): see more information on <https://spark.apache.org/docs/2.4.7/sql-data-sources-hive-tables.html>.

### application.properties

| **Property** | **Default** | **Description** |
| --- | --- | --- |
| gtemplar.application.group | Blendata | Application group information. |
| gtemplar.application.id | 1 | Application ID Information. |
| gtemplar.application.name | ZEUS | Application name information. |
| gtemplar.application.secret |  |  |
| gtemplar.application.version | 3.3.0 | Application version information. |
| gtemplar.application.description | readme.md | Application description information, in Markdown file format. |
| gtemplar.application.copyright | COPYRIGHT 2020 G-ABLE CO., LTD. | Application copyright information. |
| gtemplar.application.tenant.name | MASTER\_TENANT | Application Tenant name. |
| gtemplar.dynamic.shuffle | false |  |
| gtemplar.shuffle.ratio | 2.0 |  |
|  |  |  |
| root.home.path | ${GTEMPLAR\_HOME} | The location of Zeus home directory. |
| root.data.path | ${ZEUS\_DATA} | The location of Zeus data directory. |
|  |  |  |
| spark.appName | ZEUS | Spark application name. Should be the same name setting by property `gtemplar.application.name`. |
| spark.schedulePool | default | Pool name which all spark jobs in Zeus application submit to [[6]](#zeus-reference). |
| spark.thrift.enable | true | Flag to open Hive JDBC to access Spark SQL [[7]](#zeus-reference). |
| spark.thrift.port | 9999 | Value mapped to Spark property `hive.server2.thrift.port` as a Hive JDBC listening port [[7]](#zeus-reference). |
| #spark.hadoopConf | /hadoop/etc | Path to the directory of configuration files `core-site.xml`, `hdfs-site.xml` for **Hadoop** data storage. |
| spark.dataStorePath | ${root.data.path}/store | Path to Spark data storage. |
| dataStorePath.storeBackup | ${root.data.path}/store\_backup | Path to the directory for backup data in case users would like to delete Spark table metadata, but store data raw files. |
| dataStorePath.tmp | ${root.data.path}/tmp | Path to store the temporary files. |
| dataStorePath.trash | ${root.data.path}/trash | Path to the directory to store deleted files for **Local** data storage. For **Hadoop** data storage, this property is not applied. |
|  |  |  |
| sparkjob.monitor.url | <http://localhost:4040/api/v1> | Spark service url to get Spark jobs status. |
|  |  |  |
| management.endpoint.shutdown.enabled | true | Whether to enable the shutdown endpoint [[1]](#zeus-reference). |
| management.endpoints.web.exposure.include | \* | Select ID of monitoring and managing endpoints to expose [[1]](#zeus-reference). By Default, setting to \* means expose all endpoints. |
|  |  |  |
| server.port | 11000 | Zeus server HTTPS port [[2]](#zeus-reference). |
| server.servlet.context-path | /zeus/v1 | Zeus root context path [[2]](#zeus-reference). By default, application can be accessed from [https://localhost:11000/zeus/v1.](https://localhost:11020/cerberus/v1.) |
| server.ssl.key-store | ${root.home.path}/ssl/server.keystore.jks | Path to the key store that holds the SSL certificate to enable HTTPS protocol [[2]](#zeus-reference). You should generate a new certificate associated with public IP or domain name. |
| server.ssl.key-store-password |  | Password used to access the key in the key store [[2]](#zeus-reference). |
| server.ssl.key-password |  | Password used to access the key store [[2]](#zeus-reference). |
|  |  |  |
| spring.main.banner-mode | off | Mode used to display the banner when the application runs [[2]](#zeus-reference). By default, disable Spring banner printed on console at startup. |
| spring.datasource.url | jdbc:mysql://localhost:3306/zeus?characterEncoding | Database connection in JDBC URL format [[2]](#zeus-reference). |
| spring.datasource.username | zeus | Database login username [[2]](#zeus-reference). |
| spring.datasource.password |  | Database login password [[2]](#zeus-reference). |
| spring.datasource.driverClassName | com.mysql.jdbc.Driver | JDBC driver class name to connect to application database [[2]](#zeus-reference). |
| spring.datasource.platform | mysql | Specific database script platform [[2]](#zeus-reference) i.e. schema-mysql.sql. |
|  |  |  |
| spring.jpa.hibernate.ddl-auto | update | Hibernate DDL mode (create, create-drop, validate, update). Default to update which allows Hibernate to auto update tables schema [[2]](#zeus-reference). |
|  |  |  |
| spring.servlet.multipart.max-file-size | 209715200 | Maximum file size [[2]](#zeus-reference). By default, upload file size cannot exceed 200MB. |
| spring.servlet.multipart.max-request-size | 209715200 | Maximum request size [[2]](#zeus-reference). By default, multipart/form-data request size cannot exceed 200MB. |
|  |  |  |
| spring.mvc.async.request-timeout | 3600000 | Amount of time before asynchronous request handling times out [[2]](#zeus-reference). |
| spring.mvc.async.timeout | 3600000 | Read timeout when reading from Input stream when a connection is established to a resource [[4]](#zeus-reference). |
|  |  |  |
| filemanage.upload.path | ${root.data.path}/upload | Path to the directory of upload files. |
| filemanage.export.path | ${root.data.path}/export | Path to the directory of export files. |
| filemanage.export.compress.format | gzip | Spark ouput data compression format on exporting files. |
| filemanage.export.folder.iscompress | true | Flag to enable `zip` the ouput folder of export files. |
| filemanage.export.hash.codec | SHA-256 | Hash file codec type writing alongside export `zip` folders for validating file completion. |
| filemanage.export.external.compress.format | gzip | Spark ouput data compression format on exporting files to external sources i.e. S3, Azure, Google Cloud. |
|  |  |  |
| custom-hive.config.path | ${root.home.path}/config | Path to the directory of configuration file `custom-hive.properties` which contains additional information for Hive authentication. |
|  |  |  |
| spring.main.allow-bean-definition-overriding | true | Whether bean definition overriding, by registering a definition with the same name as an existing definition, is allowed [[2]](#zeus-reference). |
|  |  |  |
| spring.custom.service.path | ${root.home.path}/jars | Path to the directory of Zeus custom services jar files. |
| spring.custom.udf.path | ${root.home.path}/extra-jars | Path to the directory of Zeus custom User Defined Function jar files. See more information on [Blendata: How To create Blendata UDF](https://blendata.atlassian.net/wiki/spaces/KB/pages/345079809/Blendata+How+To+create+Blendata+UDF). |
|  |  |  |
| logging.level.com.gable.templar.zeus.filter.CustomRequestLoggingFilter | DEBUG | Enable logging incoming request. Comment out this property to disable logging incoming request [[7]](#zeus-reference). |
| filter.payload.enable | true | Whether to print payload in the incoming request log message [[7]](#zeus-reference). |
| filter.payload.maxLength | 60000 | Maximum length of the payload to be included in the incoming request log message [[7]](#zeus-reference). |
|  |  |  |
| jasypt.encryptor.bean | customJasyptEncryptor | Specific custom encryptor class to use for encrypting password properties in file conguration application.properties. |

### service.properties

| **Property** | **Default** | **Description** |
| --- | --- | --- |
| service.cerberus.url | <https://localhost:11020/cerberus/v1> | Cerberus service url and context path. |
| service.hera.url | <https://localhost:11010> | Her service url. |
| service.cerberus.client.id |  | The application ID registered in authorization server (Cerberus). This default value cannot be changed. |
| service.cerberus.client.secret |  | The application secret registered in authorization server (Cerberus). This default value cannot be changed. |

- Cerberus endpoints provided for each service are specified by the properties started with prefix`service.cerberus`. These properties are no need to configure.
- Hera endpoints provided for each service are specified by the properties started with prefix `hera` and `service.hera`. These properties are no need to configure.

1. Spring Boot Actuator: Production-ready Features. <https://docs.spring.io/spring-boot/docs/current/reference/html/production-ready-features.html>.

3. Class URLConnection. <https://docs.oracle.com/javase/8/docs/api/java/net/URLConnection.html>.
4. Class AbstractRequestLoggingFilter. <https://docs.spring.io/spring-framework/docs/5.1.6.RELEASE_to_5.2.0.M1/Spring Framework 5.2.0.M1/org/springframework/web/filter/AbstractRequestLoggingFilter.html>.
5. Job Scheduling. <https://spark.apache.org/docs/2.4.7/job-scheduling.html>.
6. Distributed SQL Engine. <https://spark.apache.org/docs/2.4.7/sql-distributed-sql-engine.html>.

| gtemplar.application.group | Blendata | Application group information. |

| gtemplar.application.name | BROWN-COW | Application name information. |
| gtemplar.application.version | 3.2.2 | Application version information. |
| gtemplar.application.description | readme.md | Application description information, in Markdown file format. |
| gtemplar.application.copyright | COPYRIGHT 2020 G-ABLE CO., LTD. | Application copyright information. |
| gtemplar.application.tenant.name | MASTER\_TENANT | Application Tenant name. |
|  |  |  |
| management.endpoint.shutdown.enabled | true | Whether to enable the shutdown endpoint [[1]](#bc-reference). |
| management.endpoints.web.exposure.include | \* | Select ID of monitoring and managing endpoints to expose [[1]](#bc-reference). By Default, setting to \* means expose all endpoints. |
| management.endpoint.health.show-details | always | When to show full health details [[2]](#bc-reference). |
|  |  |  |
| root.home.path | ${BC\_HOME} | Path to Brown Cow home directory. |
|  |  |  |
| server.port | 11111 | Brown Cow server HTTPS port [[2]](#bc-reference). |
| server.ssl.key-store | ${root.home.path}/ssl/server.keystore.jks | Path to the key store that holds the SSL certificate to enable HTTPS protocol [[2]](#bc-reference). You should generate a new certificate associated with public IP or domain name. |
| server.ssl.key-store-password |  | Password used to access the key in the key store [[2]](#bc-reference). |
| server.ssl.key-password |  | Password used to access the key store [[2]](#bc-reference). |
| logging.config | ${root.home.path}/config/logback.xml | The location of Brown Cow logging config file [[2]](#bc-reference). |
|  |  |  |
| spring.main.banner-mode | off | Mode used to display the banner when the application runs [[2]](#bc-reference). By default, disable Spring banner printed on console at startup. |
|  |  |  |
| spring.datasource.url | jdbc:h2:file:${root.home.path}/browncowdb | Database connection in JDBC URL format [[2]](#bc-reference). Brown Cow connect to an embed database H2, so this property points to H2 database file. |
| #spring.datasource.username | sa | Database login username [[2]](#bc-reference). |
| #spring.datasource.password |  | Database login password [[2]](#bc-reference). |
| spring.datasource.driver-class | org.h2.Driver | JDBC driver class name to connect to application database [[2]](#bc-reference). |
|  |  |  |
| spring.jpa.hibernate.ddl-auto | update | Hibernate DDL mode (create, create-drop, validate, update). Default to update which allows Hibernate to auto update tables schema [[2]](#bc-reference). |
|  |  |  |
| spring.devtools.restart.exclude | static/\*\* | Patterns that should be excluded from triggering a full restart [[2]](#bc-reference). |
|  |  |  |
| spring.h2.console.enabled | true | Whether to enable the console [[2]](#bc-reference). |
| spring.h2.console.settings.web-allow-others | true | Whether to enable remote access [[2]](#bc-reference). |
|  |  |  |
| spring.mvc.async.request-timeout | 3600000 | Amount of time before asynchronous request handling times out [[2]](#bc-reference). |
| spring.mvc.async.timeout | 21600000 | Read timeout when reading from Input stream when a connection is established to a resource [[3]](#bc-reference). |
|  |  |  |
| brown-cow.pulse.interval | 5 | Time interval (seconds) Brow Cow check the registered monitor modules, and the server resource usage status. |
| brown-cow.retention.day | 7 | The number of days that Brown Cow database records be stored i.e. table name `historical`. Retention batch is running every midnight. |

| noti.util.retentionolder | 10 | The number of days that Notification database records be stored i.e. table name `inbound`, `inbound_history`, `outbound`, `outbound_history`. Retention batch is running every midnight. |

| noti.im.ip | "localhost" | Web socket hostname or server IP. |
| noti.im.port | 10009 | Web socket server port. |

| runscript.timeout | 5000 |  |
| noti.file.upload.path | "/data/noti/" | Path to the directory to store temporary file in case send e-mail with file attachment, for example, `/data/noti_ftp`. |
