## Cerberus: Update Security Policy

| Field | Value |
|---|---|
| **URL** | https://blendata.atlassian.net/wiki/spaces/bde460/pages/2255323530/Cerberus+Update+Security+Policy |
| **Last modified** | 2025-03-26T09:08:30.972Z |
| **Breadcrumb** | Blendata Enterprise V.4.6.0 > General References > Blendata API Document > Cerberus API > Security Policy |

The system has a security policy that is used as a requirement for authentication and authorization. And you can customize it as the situation.

The system has provided an API to update the security policy that detail as below

The security policy configuration has been set as the ***default value with the PCI recommendation*****.** You can change the default configuration but we recommend you ensure need to change it.

## API Document

**Update Security Policy API**

HTTP Method : POST

{{cerberus\_url}}:{{cerberus\_port}}/{{cerberus\_rootpath}}/securityPolicy

An endpoint to update security policy of user.

## Request Params

| Parameter | Data Type | Description |
| --- | --- | --- |
| appId | number | The ID of the application that you need to update security policy. |
| config | Config | The set of security policy configurations[1]. |

**Config Params[1]**

| Parameter | Data Type | Description |
| --- | --- | --- |
| noExpiryDate | number |  |
| countLoginFailedLimit | number |  |
| reusedPasswordLimit | number |  |
| inactiveDaysLimit | number |  |
| changePasswordDays | number |  |
| loginFailedAttemptInSec | number |  |
| exclusionUsers | Exclusion Users | The set of users has been exclusion the security policies[2]. |

**Exclusion Users Params [2]**

| Parameter | Data Type | Description |
| --- | --- | --- |
| inactiveDaysLimit | Array[String] |  |
| countLoginFailedLimit | Array[String] |  |
| noExpiryDate | Array[String] |  |

**HEADERS**

```
Content-Type : application/json
```

**BODY**

```
{
    "appId": 2,
    "config": {
        "noExpiryDate": 60,
        "countLoginFailedLimit": 6,
        "reusedPasswordLimit": 4,
        "inactiveDaysLimit": 90,
        "changePasswordDays": 90,
        "loginFailedAttemptInSec": 120,
        "exclusionUsers": {
        	"inactiveDaysLimit": ["super_admin","system"],
        	"countLoginFailedLimit": ["super_admin","system"],
        	"noExpiryDate": ["super_admin","system"]
        }
    }
}
```

**EXAMPLE RESPONSE**

```
{
    "id": 5,
    "config": {
        "noExpiryDate": 60,
        "countLoginFailedLimit": 6,
        "reusedPasswordLimit": 4,
        "inactiveDaysLimit": 90,
        "changePasswordDays": 90,
        "loginFailedAttemptInSec": 120,
        "exclusionUsers": {
            "inactiveDaysLimit": [
                "super_admin",
                "system"
            ],
            "countLoginFailedLimit": [
                "super_admin",
                "system"
            ],
            "noExpiryDate": [
                "super_admin",
                "system"
            ]
        }
    },
    "appId": 2,
    "createdDate": 1584252907000,
    "createdBy": "system",
    "modifiedDate": 1584521263136,
    "modifiedBy": "super_admin",
    "version": 25
}
```

---

## Blendata Logging

| Field | Value |
|---|---|
| **URL** | https://blendata.atlassian.net/wiki/spaces/bde460/pages/2255323546/Blendata+Logging |
| **Last modified** | 2026-04-02T08:57:56.242Z |
| **Breadcrumb** | Blendata Enterprise V.4.6.0 > General References |

Blendata Enterprise applies the [Logback](https://logback.qos.ch/) and [Log4j](https://logging.apache.org/log4j/2.x/) libraries for application logging, facilitating the storage of various activities such as Authentication, Access, Import, Modify, Delete, and more. Furthermore, it allows to customize the log pattern. Each module description is per below:

- **Cerberus:** Cerberus is a module for security management including authentication, authorization, and integration with third-party authentication (LDAP).
- **Hera:** Web API Service and License Control. Provides two ways REST API and Client Web HTML(EROS), use for data integration, data management, data processing, and data utilization. Including data protection (assets) and be able to share and export.
- **Zeus (Spark Application[Driver]):** Web API to Manage and Execute Spark Jobs, which is a module designed for processing data and computing services e.g. querying, streaming, etc. As it is a Spark application, the module can use the performance of the Spark Optimization engine to process big data.
- **Notebook (Zeppelin):** REST APIs for interaction and remote activation of zeppelin functionality.
- **Spark:** Spark Application and Resource Management.
- **Notification Server:** Web API Server Manage inbound and outbound.

---

# How to Get Logs from Modules

If an error occurs and the root cause is unclear, logs are required for investigation.

**Steps:**

1. SSH into the server where the Modules is running.
2. Use the following command to view the logs. In this example, the issue occurs in the Zeus module.

   1. Navigate to the Module installation directory:

      ```
      cd /opt/blendata/zeus/
      ```
   2. Run the following command:

      ```
      tail -1000f /var/log/blendata/zeus/zeus.log
      ```

**Please note that log file paths may differ across environments depending on the server configuration.**

3. Review the log details and copy the relevant information into a text file for further support or investigation.

### **Log File Paths**

The paths below represent the default log locations; however, they may vary depending on the environment and server configuration.

|  |  |
| --- | --- |
| **Modules** | **Path Logs** |
| Cerberus | /var/log/blendata/cerberus/cerberus.log |
| Hera | /var/log/blendata/hera/hera.log |
| Spark Master | /var/log/blendata/spark |
| Spark Worker | /var/log/blendata/spark |
| Zeus | /var/log/blendata/zeus\_master/zeus.log |
| Zeppelin | /var/log/blendata/zeppelin/zeppelin.log |
| Airflow | /var/log/blendata/airflow/scheduler, /var/log/blendata/airflow/webserver |
| Workflow | /var/log/blendata/workflow-agent |

---

# Log Pattern

Able to specify the log pattern for each module in Blendata Enterprise by configuring the corresponding configuration file such as Logback.xml or log4j.properties with the default pattern for each module setting as per below:

### **Cerberus, Hera, Zeus Log Format :**

```
%d{yyyy-MM-dd'T'HH:mm:ss.sss'Z'}  %-5p [%C:%M] [%X{userId} : %X{traceId}] - %m%n
```

### **Output:**

```
2023-03-24T16:30:10.010Z  INFO  [com.gable.templar.hera.service.tableManage.TableManageServiceImpl:refreshTable] [sunisa.l : ce084642-3ad4-448e-abdb-d33d65ef1fcb] - call refresh table: kafka_filecollector_csv_mockdata_stream_time_partition_i_01 at zeusId :5
```

- `%d{yyyy-MM-dd'T'HH:mm:ss.sss'Z'}` : date and time that the event occurs likes `2023-03-24T16:30:10.010Z`.
- `%-5p` : severity of the message such as `INFO` `WARN` `ERROR`.
- `[%C:%M]` : service and function for example.

`[com.gable.templar.hera.service.tableManage.TableManageServiceImpl:refreshTable]`.

- `%X{userId}` : username likes `sunisa.l`.
- `%X{traceId}]` : unique ID per request used to refer between the modules about the event that is requested at the same time for example `ce084642-3ad4-448e-abdb-d33d65ef1fcb`.
- `%m` : the event message likes `call refresh table: kafka_filecollector_csv_mockdata_stream_time_partition_i_01 at zeusId :5`.
- `%n` : adds a newline character to the log output.

### **Zeppelin Log Format:**

```
%5p [%d] ({%t} %F[%M]:%L) - %m%n
```

### **Output:**

```
INFO [2024-10-31 12:45:07,188] ({DefaultQuartzScheduler_Worker-2} ManagedInterpreterGroup.java[close]:107) - Remove this InterpreterGroup: spark-isolated-2JK8JGJNR-2024-10-31_12-45-00 as all the sessions are closed
```

- `%5p` : severity of the message such as `INFO` `WARN` `ERROR`.
- `%d` : date and time with milliseconds.
- `%t` : the thread name, enclosed in braces {} likes `DefaultQuartzScheduler_Worker-2`.
- `%F` : The file name where the log event was generated likes `ManagedInterpreterGroup.java`.
- `%M` : name of the method such as `close`.
- `%L` : the exact line number in the source file likes `107`.
- `%m` : the event message likes `Remove this InterpreterGroup: spark-isolated-2JK8JGJNR-2024-10-31_12-45-00 as all the sessions are closed`.
- `%n` : adds a newline character to the log output.

### **Spark Master, Worker, Executor Log Format:**

```
[Thread] LogLevel ClassName - EventMessage
```

### **Output:**

```
[dispatcher-event-loop-3] INFO org.apache.spark.deploy.master.Master - Start scheduling for app app-20241028172239-0000 with rpId: 0
```

- `Thread` : thread name of Spark such as `dispatcher-event-loop-3`.
- `LogLevel` : severity of the message such as `INFO` `WARN` `ERROR`.
- `ClassName` : service and function likes `org.apache.spark.deploy.master.Master`.
- `EventMessage` : the event message likes `Start scheduling for app app-20241028172239-0000 with rpId: 0`.

### **Noti-Server Log Format:**

```
%d{yyyy-MM-dd HH:mm:ss} %-5p %-20c{1} - %M : %m%n
```

### **Output:**

```
2024-10-31 11:37:44 INFO  NotificationServer$  - run : call Shut down !
```

- `%d{yyyy-MM-dd'T'HH:mm:ss}` : date and time that the event occurs likes `2024-10-31 11:37:44`.
- `%-5p` : severity of the message such as `INFO` `WARN` `ERROR` `DEBUG`.
- `%-20c{1}` : service and function for example `NotificationServer$`.
- `%M` : method name likes `run`.
- `%m` : the event message likes `call Shut down !`.
- `%n` : adds a newline character to the log output.

## Blendata Enterprise High Availability

| Field | Value |
|---|---|
| **URL** | https://blendata.atlassian.net/wiki/spaces/bde460/pages/2255323650/Blendata+Enterprise+High+Availability |
| **Last modified** | 2025-03-26T09:08:33.545Z |
| **Breadcrumb** | Blendata Enterprise V.4.6.0 > Administrator User Manual > Related Documents |

# Overview

Blendata Enterprise is a platform that acts as a medium between data and users to streamline complex data management through a convenient all-in-one system covering data ingestion, processing, analysis, and usage readily available for all data missions of the data-driven generation.

You can divide the main functions of the platform into several parts, which include the Security Module(**Cerberus**), Management Module(**Hera**), and Compute Module(**Zeus**). Therefore, we would like to discuss High Availability separately for each module.

- Security Module(**Cerberus**) is a module for security management including authentication, authorization, and integration with third-party authentication (LDAP).
- Management Module(**Hera**) is an API server. It provides two ways REST API and Client Web HTML(EROS), used for data integration, data management, data processing, and data utilization. Including data protection (assets) and being able to share and export.
- Compute Module(**Zeus**) is a module designed for processing data and computing services e.g. querying, streaming, etc. As it is a Spark application, the module can use the performance of the Spark Optimization engine to process big data

# High Availability

I would like to discuss an important aspect of our software - High Availability (HA). Ensuring High Availability is crucial to delivering a reliable and seamless user experience.

High Availability refers to the capability of our system to continue functioning without disruption even in the face of hardware failures, software issues, or other unforeseen challenges. Here are some key considerations for achieving High Availability in our web application

- **Redundancy:** Implement redundancy by setting up multiple instances of our application on separate servers or locations. This redundancy helps in maintaining service availability even if one instance encounters issues.
- **Load Balancing**: Utilize load balancing mechanisms to distribute incoming web traffic evenly across multiple application instances. This not only enhances performance but also provides fault tolerance.
- **Data Consistency**: When multiple instances of our application share a cache server, they can access and update cached data consistently. This ensures that regardless of which instance serves a user's request, the data remains synchronized and accurate.
- **Database Replication**: Utilize database replication to create copies of our data on multiple database servers. This ensures that if one database server fails, another can seamlessly take over, reducing downtime.

## Security Module(Cerberus) and Management Module(Hera)

These modules can work by multiple working nodes with Cluster concept. This mode has high availability. If Hera or Cerberus in some node unavailabled, BDE system still availabled without down time by works with Hera or Cerberus on another node instead.

![Full HA Architecture.png](https://blendata.atlassian.net/wiki/download/attachments/2255323650/Full%20HA%20Architecture.png?api=v2)

## Compute Module(**Zeus**)

The main engine of this module is Apache Spark, so we refer to the HA concept with Spark Cluster. However, Spark Driver remains the main issue. Therefore, we have developed a feature to solve this problem. It consists of 2 topics: Realm and Group.

- **Realm** is what defines a compute node within the same Realm to work at. same set of data and metadata
- **Group** is the creation of a group of computer nodes. To help each other work For the job that is scheduled to be run at a Group

# Testing

## Prerequisite

- Management Cluster (Hera and Cerberus) at least 2 instance
- Services Cluster (MySQL, Redis, Zookeeper, Spark Master) at least 3 instance
- HPC Cluster (Zeus) at least 2 instance
- Worker Cluster (Spark Worker) at least 3 instance

## Testing Scenarios

### Management Node Failure

**Description**: Simulating the failure of one management node to observe how the Management cluster maintains Web Portal availability.

**Action**: Shutdown one management node in cluster or kill process.

**Expected Outcome**: Load balance or VIP send user request to available node

### Services Cluster

**Description**: Simulating the failure of one service node to observe how the service cluster maintains services availability.

**Action**: Shutdown one service node in cluster or kill process.

**Expected Outcome**: Platform can access meta-data in MySQL, access cache data in Redis, access zookeeper, workers have been registered to new master, and the jobs can run normally.

### HPC Cluster

**Description**: Simulating the failure of one HPC node to observe how the management maintains jobs availability.

**Action**: Create a Zeus group with more than one Zeus in management and set the Zeus group to the job. And shutdown one HPC node in cluster or kill process.

**Expected Outcome**: The job can be run normally if the Zeus group has at least 1 Zeus.

### Worker Cluster

**Description**: Simulating the failure of one Spark worker node to observe how the Spark master maintains jobs availability.

**Action**: Shutdown one worker node in cluster or kill process.

**Expected Outcome**: All Zeus work normally if enable dynamic allocation to all Zeus but if not enable dynamic allocation cannot guarantee all Zeus work normally.

# Reference

[Apache Spark](https://spark.apache.org/): [High Availability](https://spark.apache.org/docs/latest/spark-standalone.html#high-availability)

## High Availability Guide for Blendata Enterprise

| Field | Value |
|---|---|
| **URL** | https://blendata.atlassian.net/wiki/spaces/bde460/pages/2255323686/High+Availability+Guide+for+Blendata+Enterprise |
| **Last modified** | 2025-03-26T09:08:34.306Z |
| **Breadcrumb** | Blendata Enterprise V.4.6.0 > Administrator User Manual > Related Documents > Blendata Enterprise High Availability |

[data-colorid=ba4ipouten]{color:#4c9aff} html[data-color-mode=dark] [data-colorid=ba4ipouten]{color:#004eb3}[data-colorid=aoepwoclt6]{color:#4c9aff} html[data-color-mode=dark] [data-colorid=aoepwoclt6]{color:#004eb3}[data-colorid=qjn57lg5f6]{color:#4c9aff} html[data-color-mode=dark] [data-colorid=qjn57lg5f6]{color:#004eb3}[data-colorid=x7rxcdmjbw]{color:#4c9aff} html[data-color-mode=dark] [data-colorid=x7rxcdmjbw]{color:#004eb3}High Availability is supported since BDE v3.5.0 with Full HA features.

# Difference between Normal BDE and Full HA BDE

#### Normal Blendata Architecture (Standalone Mode)

Hera and Cerberus work by single working node. This mode is low availability. If Hera or Cerberus unavailable, BDE system will not available too.

![BDE Architecture.png](https://blendata.atlassian.net/wiki/download/attachments/2255323686/BDE%20Architecture.png?api=v2)

#### Full HA Blendata Architecture (Cluster Mode)

Hera and Cerberus can work by multiple working nodes with Cluster concept. This mode has high availability. If Hera or Cerberus in some node unavailabled, BDE system still availabled without down time by works with Hera or Cerberus on another node instead.

![Full HA Architecture.png](https://blendata.atlassian.net/wiki/download/attachments/2255323686/Full%20HA%20Architecture.png?api=v2)

# Supported Cluster Mode

BDE supports Cluster Mode both Active-Active and Active-Passive High-Availability Clustering

#### Active-Active High Availability Cluster

This cluster type will be controled with load balance technique. Load balancer will detect server failures and redirect client traffic automatically.

![](https://blendata.atlassian.net/wiki/download/attachments/2255323686/image-20220317-084626.png?api=v2)

#### Active-Passive High Availability Cluster

This cluster type will be controled with Fail-over technique. Fail-over is a backup operational mode in which the functions of a system node are assumed by a secondary node when the primary node becomes unavailable (through failure or scheduled down time)

![](https://blendata.atlassian.net/wiki/download/attachments/2255323686/image-20220317-084651.png?api=v2)

# How Hera and Cerberus work with Cluster mode?

Hera and Cerberus need 2 conditions to work with Cluster mode as follows:

1. Require unique address for calling from client or other modules. Such as Hera node-1 and Hera node-2 must be accessed by the same URL (From load balancer or proxy server). BDE not provides Load balance or proxy server services (Need integration for this part).  
     
    *Example:*  
    *Cerberus Node A has address as 172.20.51.201:11020*  
    *Cerberus Node B has address as 172.20.51.202:11020*  
    *Load balancer provide one address as 172.20.51.200:11020 to route request into both above addresses for Cerberus access.*  
    *Hera, Zeus or other client must call to Cerberus with 172.20.51.200:11020 only*
2. Require sharing cache in each nodes for protect data conflict in multiple node working. BDE can mange this condition by **Redis** [[1]](#full-ha-ref)server.

# How to setup BDE to cluster mode

### 1. Redis setup

BDE recommend to install Redis with Sentinal Mode for HA supported. By follow this installation guide: [How to Setup Redis For High Availability with Sentinel in CentOS 8](https://www.tecmint.com/setup-redis-high-availability-with-sentinel-in-centos-8/)

### 2. Cerberus setup

Apply configuration file `config/application.properties` as below pattern with all Cerberus nodes

```
### spring.cache.type: set cache provider type i.e. 'simple' to cache by in-memory, 'redis' to cache by Redis database
spring.cache.type=redis
spring.cache.redis.time-to-live=180000

#### Redis Sentinel configuration ###
spring.redis.sentinel.master=mymaster
spring.redis.sentinel.nodes=sentinel-ip1:26379,sentinel-ip2:26379,sentinel-ip3:26379
```

**Configuration description**

| **Property** | **Default** | **Description** |
| --- | --- | --- |
| spring.cache.type | simple | Set cache provider type   - `simple`: to set cache by in-memory. - `redis`: to set cache by Redis database. |
| spring.cache.redis.time-to-live | 180000 | If cache provider is `redis`, set cache time to live in milliseconds [[2]](#full-ha-ref). |
| spring.redis.sentinel.master | mymaster | Add this property to connect to Redis Sentinel [[2]](#full-ha-ref). Set Redis sentinel master name to store cache data. |
| spring.redis.sentinel.nodes | localhost1:26379,localhost2:26379,localhost3:26379 | Add this property to connect to Redis Sentinel [[2]](#full-ha-ref). Set Redis sentinel hosts to store cache data. |

### 3. Hera setup

3.1 Apply configuration file `config/application.properties` as below pattern with all Hera nodes

```
# spring.cache.type: set cache provider type i.e. 'simple' to cache by in-memory, 'redis' to cache by Redis database
spring.cache.type=redis
spring.cache.redis.time-to-live=180000

## spring.session.store-type: configure session store type i.e. 'none' no data-store, 'redis' store session by redis
spring.session.store-type=redis

#### Redis Sentinel configuration ###
spring.redis.sentinel.master=mymaster
spring.redis.sentinel.nodes=sentinel-ip1:26379,sentinel-ip2:26379,sentinel-ip3:26379
```

**Configuration description**

| **Property** | **Default** | **Description** |
| --- | --- | --- |
| spring.cache.type | simple | Set cache provider type   - `simple`: to set cache by in-memory. - `redis`: to set cache by Redis database. |
| spring.cache.redis.time-to-live | 180000 | If cache provider is `redis`, set cache time to live in milliseconds [[2]](#full-ha-ref). |
| spring.session.store-type | none | Configure session store type [[2]](#full-ha-ref).   - `none`: to store session by in-memory. - `redis`: to store session by redis. |
| spring.redis.sentinel.master | mymaster | Add this property to connect to Redis Sentinel [[2]](#full-ha-ref). Set Redis sentinel master name to store cache data. |
| spring.redis.sentinel.nodes | localhost1:26379,localhost2:26379,localhost3:26379 | Add this property to connect to Redis Sentinel [[2]](#full-ha-ref). Set Redis sentinel hosts to store cache data. |

3.2 Add more parameter in configuration file `config/quartz.properties` as below pattern with all Hera nodes

```
# Enable Quartz in a cluster
org.quartz.jobStore.isClustered=true
org.quartz.scheduler.instanceId=AUTO
```

**Configuration description**

| **Property** | **Description** |
| --- | --- |
| org.quartz.jobStore.isClustered | Set to “true” in order to turn on clustering features. This property must be set to “true” if you are having multiple instances of Quartz use the same set of database tables… otherwise you will experience havoc. See the configuration docs for clustering for more information. |
| org.quartz.scheduler.instanceId | Can be any string, but must be unique for all schedulers working as if they are the same ‘logical’ Scheduler within a cluster. You may use the value “AUTO” as the instanceId if you wish the Id to be generated for you. Or the value “SYS\_PROP” if you want the value to come from the system property “org.quartz.scheduler.instanceId”. |

# References

1. Redis is an open source (BSD licensed), in-memory data structure store, used as a database, cache, and message broker. <https://redis.io/>
2. Spring Common application properties. <https://docs.spring.io/spring-boot/docs/2.0.5.RELEASE/reference/html/common-application-properties.html>

## Blendata Enterprise Data Security

| Field | Value |
|---|---|
| **URL** | https://blendata.atlassian.net/wiki/spaces/bde460/pages/2255323739/Blendata+Enterprise+Data+Security |
| **Last modified** | 2025-03-26T09:08:35.603Z |
| **Breadcrumb** | Blendata Enterprise V.4.6.0 > General References |

When it comes to data protection in Blendata, it is separated into two main parts.

1. Secure data storage: Users can choose an encryption function for encrypting the data file since process import data.
2. Protecting data when in use: Users can use built-in protection data functions to protect data in access to the data. Including GUI to set security level for each data asset.

# Secure data storage

## Overview

One of the main features of Blendata Enterprise is the processing of big data. In order to process big data efficiently One of the key points is the data storage format, and Blend Data chooses to use the **Parquet** file.

**Apache Parquet** is an open source, column-oriented data file format designed for efficient data storage and retrieval. It provides efficient data compression and encoding schemes with enhanced performance to handle complex data in bulk. Parquet is available in multiple languages including Java, C++, Python, etc...

## Parquet Modular Encryption

Parquet files containing sensitive information can be protected by the modular encryption mechanism that encrypts and authenticates the file data and metadata - while allowing for a regular Parquet functionality (columnar projection, predicate pushdown, encoding and compression).

## Columnar Encryption

Parquet uses the envelope encryption practice, where file parts are encrypted with “data encryption keys” (DEKs), and the DEKs are encrypted with “master encryption keys” (MEKs). The DEKs are randomly generated by Parquet for each encrypted file/column. The MEKs are generated, stored and managed in a Key Management Service (KMS) of the user's choice.

## Encryption Extensions

Blendata Enterprise provides 2 built-in functions.

1. **Local Key**: This function will read the key from the local file system.
2. **AWS KMS**: This function will read the key from the AWS KMS.

### How to enable AWS KMS from Zeus (Compute Module)

**Configuration File:** ./zeus/config/application.properties

```
## KMS online key store
kms.use = true
kms.user.key = {Replace this with AWS User Access Key}
kms.secret.key = {Replace this with AWS User Secret Key}
kms.arn.key = {Replace this with AWS KMS ARN}
```

However, Blendata Enterprise also gives customers the option to define custom functionality to integrate their KMS.

![](https://blendata.atlassian.net/wiki/download/attachments/2255323739/0YVwQemwtcXi88dvfXMOTUMUEPDKtfWFsu7BrnqoHcmoAICWyhsL8Ma6aciWFb8GmkdS6NSsAffzIGUjSQT1NmgDUdqTtyYCS6B-cCxfFgbP02lNQn-rljzaVXx1aCgiJNZuba1uiDTzGBaKMjrdU6Q?api=v2)

# Protecting data when in use

Blendata Enterprise provides 3 solutions for protecting data.

1. Feature Column-Level Security
2. SQL Function

### Feature Column-Level Security

Blendata Enterprise provides a GUI for setting column-level security to protect data. by the data owner Or people who have permission to manage data can determine to what level of protection the data in which columns are to be protected. It can be set at 2 levels, confidential and secret, as well as being able to choose where the role should be applied.

![](https://blendata.atlassian.net/wiki/download/attachments/2255323739/otchwS6yIZmlSZjuYRiY-cXpPWV8ye3rUDjoNla2n5N3cwCSZPvQeQnLtjRy11njsRyHgAY7wWe-_dwudCmZ-bUco1aH8Q2ib_QmlQGPaROlYHr-M4GyOyHvTjRIHOlyi8_Ecf24meaFO6S3Y6Tjk44?api=v2)

### SQL Function

Blendata Enterprise uses Apache Spark as core compute engine which inherently adopted SparkSQL for data processing with SQL-like language, plus rich data wrangling functions like string manipulation, mathematics, window function, etc. called SparkSQL functions. Moreover, customers can customize their function with User-defined function (UDF) that can be called in the sql query command.

Furthermore, to ensure the security of data, Blendata enterprise provides enterprise grade security such as following functions.

#### Example Built-in SQL Functions

Hash : hash(expr1, expr2, ...) - Returns a hash value of the arguments.

```
> SELECT hash('Spark', array(123), 2);
 -1321691492
```

Ref: <https://spark.apache.org/docs/latest/api/sql/#hash>

SHA: sha2(expr, bitLength) - Returns a checksum of SHA-2 family as a hex string of `expr`. SHA-224, SHA-256, SHA-384, and SHA-512 are supported. Bit length of 0 is equivalent to 256.

```
> SELECT sha2('Spark', 256);
 529bc3b07127ecb7e53a4dcf1991d9152c24537d919178022b2c42657f79a26b
```

Ref: <https://spark.apache.org/docs/latest/api/sql/#sha2>

#### Example User-Defined Functions (UDFs)

User-Defined Functions (UDFs) are a feature of Spark SQL that allows users to define their own functions when the system’s built-in functions are not enough to perform the desired task.

```
val encrypt = udf((text:String, key: String, initVector:String) => {
 val iv = new IvParameterSpec(initVector.getBytes("UTF-8"))
 val skeySpec = new SecretKeySpec(key.getBytes("UTF-8"), "AES")
 val cipher = Cipher.getInstance("AES/CBC/PKCS5PADDING")
 cipher.init(Cipher.ENCRYPT_MODE, skeySpec, iv)
 val encrypted = cipher.doFinal(text.getBytes())
 Base64.getEncoder().encodeToString(encrypted)
})

val decrypt = udf((text: String, key: String, initVector:String) => {
 val iv = new IvParameterSpec(initVector.getBytes("UTF-8"))
 val skeySpec = new SecretKeySpec(key.getBytes("UTF-8"), "AES")
 val cipher = Cipher.getInstance("AES/CBC/PKCS5PADDING")
 cipher.init(Cipher.DECRYPT_MODE, skeySpec, iv)
 val original = cipher.doFinal(Base64.getDecoder.decode(text))
 new String(original)
})

val masking = udf((text: String) => {
 val maskStr = "XXXXX"
 if(text.length > maskStr.length){
   text.substring(0, text.length-maskStr.length) + maskStr
 }else{
   maskStr
 }
})
```

#### Row and Column Level Security

Blendata Enterprise provides built-in User-Defined Functions (UDFs) for controlling the level of data security in rows and columns.

**SQL function**

- is\_user(<username:STRING>) => return true/false when input value compares with USER login.
- is\_role(<role:STRING>) => return true/false when input value contains in Roles of USER login.
- get\_user() => return username of USER login.
- get\_roles() => return string set of roles of USER login. (ex. role1,role2,role3)

**Sample SQL**

```
select case when is_role('SUPER_ADMIN') then schoolname 
            else hash(schoolname) end schoolname
      , yearterm, age, level, total
from school
```

As SQL, save it to the **view table** and share the **view table** to other users. Users who have the ‘SUPER\_ADMIN’ role only can access the raw data of the ‘schoolname’ column, another one will be accessed with the hash data.

# References

[Apache Parquet](https://parquet.apache.org/): [Parquet Modular Encryption](https://github.com/apache/parquet-format/blob/master/Encryption.md)

| **Breadcrumb** | Blendata Enterprise V.4.6.0 > General References |

## Custom visualization

After downloading Blendata package and install Hera modules from the previous page, you can custom visualization by the following steps.

1. Make visualization directory

First, make a directory in your Hera modules install under the **/web/assets/js/custom-chart** directory for the visualize you are going to create. I’m going to name the app “stock”. I will make the following directory: **/web/assets/js/custom-chart/stock**

2. Make config.json

Now, lets make the config.json file. The example contents will just be this:

```
[
    {"id": "stock","name": "stock", "backgroundimgsrc": "assets/js/custom-chart/stock/candlestick-background.svg"
      , "imgsrc": "assets/js/custom-chart/stock/candlestick-inactive.svg"
      , "activeimgsrc": "assets/js/custom-chart/stock/candlestick-active.png",
      "isMultiTrace":false,
        "scripts":[ 
          {"name":"highstock","src":"assets/js/custom-chart/stock/highstock.js","type":"text/javascript"},
          {"name":"style","src":"assets/js/custom-chart/stock/highstock.css","type":"text/css"},
          {"name":"index","src":"assets/js/custom-chart/stock/index.js","type":"text/javascript"},
          {"name":"common","src":"assets/js/custom-chart/common.js","type":"text/javascript"}
         ],
        "Data": [
          {"id": "stockdate", "placeholder": "Date *","require": true, "allowdrilldown": true, "params": [
            {"placeholder": "","type": "field","range":"","ref":"query.stockdate","value":[]}
          ]},
           {"id": "open", "placeholder": "Open Price *","require": true, "params": [
             {"placeholder": "","type": "field","range":"","ref":"query.open","value":[]}
           ]},
           {"id": "high", "placeholder": "High Price *","require": true, "params": [
            {"placeholder": "","type": "field","range":"","ref":"query.high","value":[]}
           ]},
           {"id": "low", "placeholder": "Low Price *","require": true, "params": [
            {"placeholder": "","type": "field","range":"","ref":"query.low","value":[]}
           ]},
           {"id": "close", "placeholder": "Close Price *","require": true, "params": [
            {"placeholder": "","type": "field","range":"","ref":"query.close","value":[]}
           ]},
           {"id": "sort", "placeholder": "Sorting", "params": [
             {"placeholder": "","type": "sort","range":"","ref":"query.sort","value":[]}
           ]},
           {"id": "filters", "placeholder": "Filters", "params": [
             {"placeholder": "","type": "filter","range":"","ref":"query.filter","value":[]}
           ]},
           {"id": "limits", "placeholder": "Limit", "params": [
             {"placeholder": "Number","type": "String","range":"","ref":"query.limit","value": "200"}
           ]}
         ],
         "Style": [
           {"id": "stock_option", "placeholder": "Stock Option", "type": "localStyle", "params": [
            {"placeholder": "units","type": "Select","range":"","ref":"localStyle.units","value":"day", "selecter":["day", "week","month"]}
           ]}
         ]
       }
]
```

### General keys

| **Property** | **Default** | **Description** |
| --- | --- | --- |
| id |  | ID of config |
| name |  | Name of config |
| backgroundimgsrc |  | Background image for visualize |
| imgsrc |  | Icon image for visualize type |
| activeimgsrc |  | Icon image for visualize type when selected |
| isMultiTrace |  | Flag for allow add multi traces on visualize design page |

### Scripts keys

| **Property** | **Default** | **Description** |
| --- | --- | --- |
| name |  | Name of script |
| src |  | Script url or declaration variable e.g.  {"name":"var","src": "var mxBasePath = '../../../../assets/js/custom-chart/topology';","type":""} |
| type |  | Script type e.g.   - text/javascript - text/css |

### Data keys

| **Property** | **Default** | **Description** |
| --- | --- | --- |
| id |  | ID of data |
| placeholder |  | Label for display in design screen above drop field input panel |
| require | true | Enable/Disable validate require field in design screen |
| allowdrilldown | true | Enable/Disable show display this field on drilldown config screen |
| ref |  | Field name for system execute query data |
| value |  | Default value |

### Style keys

| **Property** | **Default** | **Description** |
| --- | --- | --- |
| id |  | ID of style |
| placeholder |  | Label for display in design screen above option value |
| type | localStyle | Type of style |

### Style sub keys params

| **Property** | **Default** | **Description** |
| --- | --- | --- |
| placeholder |  | Description for input text box of style value |
| type |  | Type of style   - Select - ColorPicker - String - Number - Switch - Boolean |
| ref |  | Reference key of option |
| Value |  | Default value |
| Selecter |  | Options value for type Select e.g.  ["top", "bottom", "left", "right"] |

3. Make highstock.js

Now, lets make the highstock.js file. Download from <https://code.highcharts.com/highcharts.js>

4. Make highstock.css

Now, lets make the highstock.css file. The example contents will just be this:

```
.highcharts-candlestick-series .highcharts-point {
	stroke: red;
	fill: red;
}
.highcharts-candlestick-series .highcharts-point-up {
	stroke: green;
	fill: green;
}
```

5. Make index.js

Now, lets make the index.js file. The example contents will just be this:

```
var stock = {};//declaration variable name must equal to config id.
stock.resize = function (config) {
    stock.render(config);
}
stock.render = function (config) {
    //get container id
    let parent = $('#' + config.id);
    //clear container
    parent.empty();
    let traces = getOption(config);
    //start your customization will just be this:
    let units = '';
    let data = [];
    for (let trace of traces) {
        units = trace.localStyle.units || "day";
        for (let row of trace.dataPoints) {
            data.push([+row[trace.stockdate], +row[trace.open], +row[trace.high], +row[trace.low], +row[trace.close]]);
        }
    }
    // create the chart
    Highcharts.stockChart(config.id, {
        chart: {
            events: {
                click: function (event) {
                    var date = Highcharts.dateFormat('%Y-%m-%d', event.xAxis[0].value);
                    WidgetDrillDown({ id: config.id, data: { stockdate: date }, event: 'click' });
                }
            }
        },
        rangeSelector: {
            selected: 1
        },
        title: {
            text: 'AAPL Stock Price'
        },
        series: [{
            type: 'candlestick',
            name: 'AAPL Stock Price',
            data: data,
            dataGrouping: {
                units: [
                    [
                        units, // unit name
                        [1] // allowed multiples
                    ], [
                        'month',
                        [1, 2, 3, 4, 6]
                    ]
                ]
            }
        }]
    });

    function getOption(config) {
        try {
            let results = [];
            for (let trace of config.design.traces) {
                let result = {
                    dataPoints: trace.data.rows,
                    localStyle: getDatasetStyles(trace.styles)
                };
                for (let field of getFieldDefinition(trace.configs)) {
                    result[field.id] = field.name;
                }
                results.push(result);
            }
            return results;
        } catch (e) {
            alert(e.message);
        }
    }

    function getFieldDefinition(configs) {
        let reserved = ['sort', 'filters', 'limits'];
        let results = [];
        for (let config of configs) {
            if (!reserved.includes(config.id)) {
                for (let param of config.params) {
                    if (param.value) {
                        let value = param.value;
                        if (!!value && value.length > 0) {
                            results.push({ id: config.id, name: value[0].alias ? value[0].alias : value[0].col_name });
                        }
                    }
                }
            }
        }
        return results;
    }

    function getDatasetStyles(traceStyles) {
        let dataset = {};
        let localStyle = traceStyles.find(style => style.type === 'localStyle');
        if (!!localStyle) {
            for (let param of localStyle.params) {
                if (param.value) {
                    let refArr = param.ref.split(".");
                    let valueArr = param.value.toString().includes(",") ? CSVtoArray(param.value) : null;
                    let value = param.value;
                    if (!!valueArr && valueArr.length) {
                        value = [];
                        for (let v of valueArr) {
                            if (param.type === 'Number') {
                                value.push(parseInt(v.trim()));
                            } else {
                                value.push(v.trim());
                            }
                        }
                    }
                    dataset[refArr[1]] = (param.type === 'Number' ? parseInt(value) : value);
                }
            }
        }
        return dataset;
    }
}
```

The WidgetDrillDown method for implement drill down to another visualize on event click or dbclick.

6. Create background image and icons for Blendata visualize design page

Now, lets make the background image and icon .png or .svg file. The example contents will just be this:

stock-active.svg width: 150px; height: 150px;

stock-inactive.svg width: 150px; height: 150px;

stock-background.svg width: 1000px; height: 600px;

7. Images for finally visualize can see in design page

![](https://blendata.atlassian.net/wiki/download/attachments/1816727385/image-20210729-051336.png?api=v2)

---

## Google Sheet Configuration

| Field | Value |
|---|---|
| **URL** | https://blendata.atlassian.net/wiki/spaces/bde460/pages/2255323841/Google+Sheet+Configuration |
| **Last modified** | 2025-09-12T09:08:03.245Z |
| **Breadcrumb** | Blendata Enterprise V.4.6.0 > Administrator User Manual > Post-Installation > Related Document |

See more details, go to [Administrator - Google Sheet Configuration](https://blendata.atlassian.net/wiki/spaces/bde460/pages/2255323841/Google+Sheet+Configuration).

# Create a Google Key

1. Go to <https://cloud.google.com/>and log in with your email.
2. Click “**Console**“ menu.
3. Create your new project by clicking “**New Project**“.
4. Enable Google Sheet API.

   1. Click “**ENABLE APIS AND SERVICES**“ button.
   2. Select “**Google Sheets API**“ option and and click “**Enable**“ option.

![](https://blendata.atlassian.net/wiki/download/attachments/2255323841/gg1.png?api=v2)

5. Go to “**OAuth consent screen**” and select “External“ option for User Type.
6. Click “**Create**“ and fill in the application details such as App name, User Support Email, and Authorized domains. Then, click “**SAVE AND CONTINUE**“.
7. Click “**ADD OR REMOVE SCOPE**“ button, search for Google Sheets API, and click “**SAVE AND CONTINUE**“.

![](https://blendata.atlassian.net/wiki/download/attachments/2255323841/gs5.png?api=v2)

9. Go to “**Credentials**“. Click “**CREATE CREDENTIALS**“ and select “**OAuth client ID**“. After that, select your application type. (Blendata is a Web application).
10. Fill in a **name** and **Authorized redirect URIs**. (IP address of Hera) Then, click “**Save**“.
11. Your client ID number is generated. The users need to download Json file into your device.

![](https://blendata.atlassian.net/wiki/download/attachments/2255323841/gs9.png?api=v2)

---

# Setup Zeus configuration

1. Go to **application.properties** file.
2. Add a configuration into this file to link with a google sheet key file.

   1. google.key.path=${filemanage.credential.path}/**Key file name.json**: Fill in the name of client ID Json file.
   2. redirect.google.url=**domain/IP address**:**port number**/: Fill in your domain URL.

**Note**: For HDFS, need to upload a key to the HDFS server and then, the users need to setup a configuration in the application.properties file.

- temp.google.key.path=/local\_path/google\_cloud\_key/google\_client\_key.json
- google.key.trash.path=/local\_path/tmp/google\_cloud\_key

---

## Vulnerability Management

| Field | Value |
|---|---|
| **URL** | https://blendata.atlassian.net/wiki/spaces/bde460/pages/2255323875/Vulnerability+Management |
| **Last modified** | 2025-03-26T09:08:37.994Z |
| **Breadcrumb** | Blendata Enterprise V.4.6.0 > General References |

**BLENDATA's Commitment**: BLENDATA is dedicated to upholding the highest standards of software security and data integrity in the development and maintenance of Blendata Enterprise. This is supported by a robust Vulnerability Management Policy, aimed at proactively identifying, assessing, and mitigating vulnerabilities that could impact our products and clients.

## Roles and Responsibilities

- **Product Team**: Responsible for implementing the Vulnerability Management Policy during the development and maintenance of Blendata Enterprise, including conducting regular security reviews, vulnerability scans, and remediation activities. The team also coordinates penetration testing and source code analysis using tools like SonarQube.
- **Quality Assurance Team**: Works in tandem with the Product Team to integrate security considerations into testing methodologies.
- **Management Team**: Ensures the provision of necessary resources and supports the enforcement of the policy across the company.

## Identification of Vulnerabilities

- **Automated Source Code Scanning**: Utilizing SonarQube, an industry-leading tool, the Product Team conducts automated scans of the source code. This helps in early detection of security vulnerabilities, code smells, and bugs.
- **Annual Penetration Testing**: Once a year, external security experts are engaged to conduct penetration testing at customer sites where Blendata Enterprise is deployed. This simulates real-world attack scenarios to uncover any potential vulnerabilities that might be exploited by malicious actors.
- **Feedback from Product Usage**: Regular feedback from users and clients is monitored for any indications of security issues or vulnerabilities that might arise during the normal use of Blendata Enterprise.

## Assessment of Identified Vulnerabilities

Once a vulnerability is identified, it undergoes a thorough assessment process:

- **Initial Triage**: The Product Team performs an initial triage to determine the urgency and impact of the vulnerability. This includes assessing the ease of exploitation, the potential impact on data integrity and confidentiality, and the affected components of Blendata Enterprise.
- **Risk Assessment**: Each identified vulnerability is then subjected to a risk assessment. The risk level is determined based on factors like severity, exploitability, and the potential impact on users and business operations.
- **Classification**: Vulnerabilities are classified into categories such as Critical, High, Medium, or Low based on their assessed risk. This classification helps in prioritizing remediation efforts.

### Criteria for Prioritization

The prioritization of vulnerabilities is based on several key criteria:

- **Severity of the Vulnerability**: Assessed based on the potential impact on system integrity, confidentiality, and availability. Critical vulnerabilities that could lead to severe data breaches or system compromise are given the highest priority.
- **Exploitability**: The ease with which a vulnerability can be exploited is a significant factor. Vulnerabilities with known exploits in the wild or those that can be exploited without advanced technical skills are prioritized.
- **Impact on Business and Users**: Consideration is given to the potential business impact, including data loss, legal and compliance implications, and effect on user trust and experience.
- **Environmental Factors**: The specific context within Blendata Enterprise where the vulnerability exists, such as its operational environment and the nature of the data it handles.

### Prioritization Process

1. **Risk Rating Assignment**: Each identified vulnerability is assigned a risk rating based on the above criteria.
2. **Consultation with Relevant Teams**: The Product Team consults with the Developers and Quality Assurance teams to understand the technical and business context of the vulnerability.
3. **Development of a Remediation Plan**: A remediation plan is formulated, which includes the proposed fix, resources required, and a timeline for resolution.
4. **Resource Allocation**: Resources are allocated based on the priority of the vulnerability, ensuring that the most critical vulnerabilities are addressed first.

## Remediation and Mitigation

- The Product Team is responsible for developing and deploying fixes or mitigations for identified vulnerabilities according to their severity and potential impact.
- Critical and high-severity vulnerabilities are prioritized for immediate action.

### Remediation Timeframes

- **Critical Vulnerabilities**: Addressed immediately, with fixes deployed as soon as possible, typically within days.
- **High Severity**: Remediated within a short timeframe, usually within weeks.
- **Medium and Low Severity**: Scheduled for remediation in regular update cycles, unless their context elevates their priority.

## Vulnerability Remediation Strategies

### Immediate Remediation

For critical vulnerabilities, especially those that pose an immediate threat to system security or data integrity, BLENDATA takes swift action:

- **Emergency Patches**: Rapid development and deployment of patches to fix critical vulnerabilities.
- **Hotfixes**: In cases where a full patch cannot be immediately released, hotfixes may be applied to temporarily mitigate the risk.

### Scheduled Remediation

For vulnerabilities of lower severity, scheduled remediation is integrated into the regular development cycle:

- **Patch Releases**: Vulnerabilities are fixed in the regular update and patch release cycle of Blendata Enterprise.
- **Version Upgrades**: Some fixes are bundled into planned version upgrades of the software, especially if they require significant changes or improvements.

## Documentation and Tracking

- **Tracking and Reporting**: The progress in addressing each vulnerability is tracked meticulously. Regular reports are generated to provide updates to the management team and relevant stakeholders.

## Collaborative Assessment

- **Engagement with Developers**: The Product Team works closely with the Developers, especially after the identification of a vulnerability, to understand its context and potential impact on the system.
- **Involvement of Quality Assurance**: The Quality Assurance Team plays a crucial role in replicating vulnerabilities and validating their presence as well as the effectiveness of fixes.

## Tools and Techniques

- **SonarQube**: Detailed descriptions of how SonarQube is used, including configurations, scanning schedules, and integration with the development pipeline.
- **Penetration Testing Methodologies**: Outline the methodologies and standards followed during penetration testing, such as OWASP Top Ten, to ensure comprehensive coverage.

## Continuous Improvement

- **Feedback Loop**: Insights gained from vulnerability assessments feed into a continuous improvement loop, enhancing both the security of Blendata Enterprise and the effectiveness of the vulnerability management process.
- **Adjustment of Tools and Techniques**: Regular reviews of tools and methodologies are conducted to ensure they remain effective and aligned with current best practices and emerging threats.

## Review and Update of Policy

- This policy is subject to an annual review by the Product Team to ensure its effectiveness and alignment with evolving security standards and business needs.
- Amendments to the policy will be made in response to new security challenges, technological advancements, or regulatory changes.

---

## Dynamic Allocation for Spark Application

| Field | Value |
|---|---|
| **URL** | https://blendata.atlassian.net/wiki/spaces/bde460/pages/2255323932/Dynamic+Allocation+for+Spark+Application |
| **Last modified** | 2025-03-26T09:08:38.771Z |
| **Breadcrumb** | Blendata Enterprise V.4.6.0 > Administrator User Manual > Related Documents |

Spark can allocate resources according to the Spark Application request. The default request typically asks for a static number of resources. However, Spark also set up resources request using Dynamic Allocation. With Dynamic Allocation, the Spark cluster retrieve resources after the Spark application no longer requires them for a specified duration. Furthermore, the Spark application can automatically request additional resources based on workload tasks, subject to specific maximum resource settings or the availability of resources from the Spark Cluster.

However, Dynamic Allocation affects the overhead time, which arises from monitoring and adjusting the allocation of resources.

# Configuration and Setup

The Dynamic Allocation need an essential configuration, `spark.dynamicAllocation.enabled` need to be set as `true`, and there are two options that need to be configured as follows.

- `spark.dynamicAllocation.shuffleTracking.enabled`: set this as `true`, which allows dynamic allocation without the need for an external shuffle service. This option will try to keep alive executors that are storing shuffle data for active jobs.
- `spark.shuffle.service.enabled` : Set this as `true`.

There are other basic configurations that need to be set up according to your application, as shown in the table below.

| **Property Name** | **Default** | **Meaning** | **Since Version** |
| --- | --- | --- | --- |
| `spark.dynamicAllocation.executorIdleTimeout` | 60s | If dynamic allocation is enabled and an executor has been idle for more than this duration, the executor will be removed. For more detail, see this [description](https://spark.apache.org/docs/3.3.4/job-scheduling.html#resource-allocation-policy). | 1.2.0 |
| `spark.dynamicAllocation.maxExecutors` | infinity | Upper bound for the number of executors if dynamic allocation is enabled. | 1.2.0 |
| `spark.dynamicAllocation.minExecutors` | 0 | Lower bound for the number of executors if dynamic allocation is enabled. | 1.2.0 |
| `spark.dynamicAllocation.executorAllocationRatio` | 1 | By default, the dynamic allocation will request enough executors to maximize the parallelism according to the number of tasks to process. While this minimizes the latency of the job, with small tasks this setting can waste a lot of resources due to executor allocation overhead, as some executor might not even do any work. This setting allows to set a ratio that will be used to reduce the number of executors w.r.t. full parallelism. Defaults to 1.0 to give maximum parallelism. 0.5 will divide the target number of executors by 2 The target number of executors computed by the dynamicAllocation can still be overridden by the `spark.dynamicAllocation.minExecutors` and `spark.dynamicAllocation.maxExecutors` settings | 2.4.0 |
| `spark.dynamicAllocation.schedulerBacklogTimeout` | 1s | If dynamic allocation is enabled and there have been pending tasks backlogged for more than this duration, new executors will be requested. For more detail, see this [description](https://spark.apache.org/docs/3.3.4/job-scheduling.html#resource-allocation-policy). | 1.2.0 |

# Resource Allocation Policy

When using Dynamic Allocation, a Spark Application will need to create an Executor when there is a pending task in the waiting queue and will remove an idle Executor when they have been idle for more than a specific duration.

## Request Policy

A Spark application with dynamic allocation enabled requests more executors when it has pending tasks waiting to be scheduled. This means that the current executors is insufficient to handle all submitted tasks at once. Spark requests executors in rounds. The initial request is triggered when there have been pending tasks for `spark.dynamicAllocation.schedulerBacklogTimeout`seconds. If the queue of pending tasks persists, subsequent requests are triggered every `schedulerBacklogTimeout`seconds. Moreover, the number of executors requested in each round increases exponentially compared to the previous round. For instance, an application will add 1 executor in the first round, and then 2, 4, 8 and so on executors in the subsequent rounds.

The motivation behind the exponential increase policy is twofold. Firstly, it allows an application to be cautious when initially requesting executors, similar to the TCP slow start mechanism, in case only a few additional executors are needed. Secondly, it enables the application to efficiently ramp up its resource usage if it turns out that many executors are indeed required.

## Remove Policy

The removing executors policy is simple. A Spark application removes an executor when it has been idle for more than `spark.dynamicAllocation.executorIdleTimeout` seconds.

Note that, under most situations, this condition is mutually exclusive with the request conditionIn, most cases, if there are pending tasks to be scheduled, an executor should not be idle.

You can see full configuration in this [link](https://spark.apache.org/docs/3.3.4/configuration.html#dynamic-allocation).

Reference: <https://spark.apache.org/docs/3.3.4/configuration.html#dynamic-allocation>

---

## Airflow Load Test Benchmark Report

| Field | Value |
|---|---|
| **URL** | https://blendata.atlassian.net/wiki/spaces/bde460/pages/2255323958/Airflow+Load+Test+Benchmark+Report |
| **Last modified** | 2025-03-26T09:08:38.948Z |
| **Breadcrumb** | Blendata Enterprise V.4.6.0 > General References |

## **Objectives**

The initial primary objectives of this load testing on Airflow are to evaluate the performance of Airflow when executing tasks in parallel, monitor the performance of the system resources usage, and utilize the collected numerical results from load test to estimate the specifications of the hardware.

## **Test Scenarios, Test Cases, and Test Preparation**

To correspond with the core objectives of Airflow load test, the initial test scenarios applied include the following:

1. Triggering Multiple DAGs with 1 task within each DAG
2. Triggering 1 DAG with multiple tasks
3. Triggering Multiple DAGs with multiple tasks (2) within each DAG

These three scenarios enhance the similar target which is to ensure the performance of executing tasks concurrently on Airflow, but in different aspects since the performances between running multiple DAGs with 1 task within each DAG, running 1 DAG with multiple tasks, and running multiple DAGs with multiple tasks within each DAG could be slightly different even if the numbers of tasks are equal in overall.

For test scenarios preparation, the main components needed to be prepared in all scenarios are the successfully deployed task(s) and trigger schedule time in cron form. In each scenario, the core differences are the number of tasks within each DAG and the number of DAG. For multiple DAGs cases, the test executor has to prepare 2 groups of DAGs deploying in different time schedules but overlapping each other in order to also test the triggerer.

Within the stated test scenarios, two major sub test cases identified to oversee the state of system resource usages and examine factors that could possibly affect the performance of executing tasks in parallel on Airflow are the following:

1. Memory Scaling
2. CPU Scaling

To add on from that, default RAM scaling has been included as the third additional case to the third test scenario: Triggering Multiple DAGs with multiple tasks (2) within each DAG to reassure whether this value affects the number of parallelism.

For test cases preparation, the test executor has to scale the system resources in proper proportion, then reboot the server to apply a new set up to the server.

## **Hardware Specifications**

- 1 x HP ProLiant DL380 G7 with VMWare ESXi 6.0

  - 2 x Intel(R) Xeon(R) CPU X5675 @ 3.07GHz
  - Hyperthreaded cores
  - Memory 128GB
  - 3 x SAS 3TB
  - 4 x NIC 1Gbps

## **Set Up**

- 1 Blendata Enterprise virtual machine

  - Operating system: CentOS 7
  - Cores: 8 vCPUs
  - Memory: 16 GB
  - Storage: 80 GB
- 1 Airflow virtual machines

  - Operating system: CentOS 7
  - Initial cores: 8 vCPUs
  - Initial memory: 16 GB
  - Storage: 80 GB

![airflow.png](https://blendata.atlassian.net/wiki/download/attachments/2255323958/airflow.png?api=v2)

## **Test Results**

### **Test Scenario 1: Triggering Multiple DAGS with 1 task in each DAG**

The virtualizations overall represent the maximum number of tasks from *multiple* DAGs that could be executed in parallel all at once in different hardware specifications by memory and CPU scaling.

#### Case 1: Memory Scaling

![airflow1.png](https://blendata.atlassian.net/wiki/download/attachments/2255323958/airflow1.png?api=v2)

#### Case 2: CPU Scaling

![airflow2.png](https://blendata.atlassian.net/wiki/download/attachments/2255323958/airflow2.png?api=v2)

### **Test Scenario 2: Triggering 1 DAG with Concurrent Tasks**

The following bar charts represent the maximum number of tasks within a *single* DAG that could be executed in parallel all at once in different hardware specifications based on memory and CPU scaling.

#### Case 1: Memory Scaling

![airflow3.png](https://blendata.atlassian.net/wiki/download/attachments/2255323958/airflow3.png?api=v2)

#### Case 2: CPU Scaling

![airflow4.png](https://blendata.atlassian.net/wiki/download/attachments/2255323958/airflow4.png?api=v2)

### **Test Scenario 3: Triggering Multiple DAGs with Concurrent Tasks**

The graphs below demonstrate the maximum number of tasks from multiple DAGs that could be executed in parallel all at once in different hardware specifications by memory, CPU, and default RAM scaling.

#### Case 1: Memory Scaling

![airflow5.png](https://blendata.atlassian.net/wiki/download/attachments/2255323958/airflow5.png?api=v2)

#### Case 2: CPU Scaling

![airflow6.png](https://blendata.atlassian.net/wiki/download/attachments/2255323958/airflow6.png?api=v2)

#### Case 3: Default RAM Scaling

![airflow7.png](https://blendata.atlassian.net/wiki/download/attachments/2255323958/airflow7.png?api=v2)

## **Summary**

From all test cases collected results, it could be narrowed down to the following conclusions:

1. The performance of parallel execution is influenced by the number of tasks being processed concurrently, not the number of DAGs executed at the moment.
2. The most significant factor that affects the number of tasks that could be performed in parallel is the memory size.
3. CPU size could be one of the factors that affects the parallel execution performance of Airflow when the memory size is limited.
4. The Default RAM value does not affect the number of parallel tasks or DAGs be executed all at once at the same time.

---

## Blendata Command Line Interface

| Field | Value |
|---|---|
| **URL** | https://blendata.atlassian.net/wiki/spaces/bde460/pages/2255324015/Blendata+Command+Line+Interface |
| **Last modified** | 2026-03-02T07:47:42.469Z |
| **Breadcrumb** | Blendata Enterprise V.4.6.0 > General References |

Blendata CLI is an application used to connect to the HERA Application via the command line. If you have not installed Blendata CLI yet, please proceed to the [Blendata CLI Installation](https://blendata.atlassian.net/wiki/x/AgC_sQ) page.

![CLI.png](https://blendata.atlassian.net/wiki/download/attachments/2255324015/CLI.png?api=v2)

# Functions and Commands

![](https://blendata.atlassian.net/wiki/images/icons/grey_arrow_down.png)Login

Login to Blendata system with password file. There are login options as below:

- **-u** : user name
- **-p** : password
- **-pf** : password file location
- **-H** : HERA ip address
- **-P** : HERA port (Optional : default value is 11010)
- **-t** : tenant name (Optional)
- **-r** : realm name (Optional)
- **-z** : zeus name (Optional)

```
bld-cli -u  <user name> -p <password> -H <host of Hera url> -P <port> [-t <tenant name>] [-r <realm name>] [-z <zeus name>];
```

```
bld-cli -u  <user name> -pf <password file location> -H <host of Hera url> -P <port> [-t <tenant name>] [-r <realm name>] [-z <zeus name>];
```

**Example:**

```
bld-cli -u John -p “p1a2s3s4” -H “heradomain.com” -P 8080;
```

- Login to system (Specify the realm name with option -r )

![](https://blendata.atlassian.net/wiki/download/attachments/2255324015/image-20230330-061941.png?api=v2)

- Login to system (Specify the zeus name with option -z)

![](https://blendata.atlassian.net/wiki/download/attachments/2255324015/image-20230330-062313.png?api=v2)

- Login to system (Specify the tenant name with option -t)

![](https://blendata.atlassian.net/wiki/download/attachments/2255324015/image-20230330-062400.png?api=v2)

![](https://blendata.atlassian.net/wiki/images/icons/grey_arrow_down.png)All Commands

This command uses to show all commands.

```
help;
```

![command list.png](https://blendata.atlassian.net/wiki/download/attachments/2255324015/command%20list.png?api=v2)

![](https://blendata.atlassian.net/wiki/images/icons/grey_arrow_down.png)View usage of each command or subcommand

This command uses to show a command usage and description.

```
command -h;
```

**Example:**

```
list -h;
```

![](https://blendata.atlassian.net/wiki/download/attachments/2255324015/image-20230330-055022.png?api=v2)

```
list tables -h;
```

![](https://blendata.atlassian.net/wiki/download/attachments/2255324015/image-20230330-055041.png?api=v2)

![](https://blendata.atlassian.net/wiki/images/icons/grey_arrow_down.png)Show Command

This command uses to display connection details such as user, host, realm, tenant, and zeus.

```
show connect-info;
```

![](https://blendata.atlassian.net/wiki/download/attachments/2255324015/image-20230330-055113.png?api=v2)

![](https://blendata.atlassian.net/wiki/images/icons/grey_arrow_down.png)Use Command

This command uses to change Realm and Zeus.

1. **Realm**

```
use realm [-i <realm id>] [-n <realm name>];
```

**Example:**

```
use realm -n no_zeus;
use realm -i 2;
```

![](https://blendata.atlassian.net/wiki/download/attachments/2255324015/image-20230330-055545.png?api=v2)

2. **Zeus**

```
use zeus [-i <zeus id>] [-n <zeus name>];
```

**Example:**

```
use zeus -i 26;
use zeus -n ZEUS_A;
```

![](https://blendata.atlassian.net/wiki/download/attachments/2255324015/image-20230330-055628.png?api=v2)

![](https://blendata.atlassian.net/wiki/images/icons/grey_arrow_down.png)List Command

This command uses to display Realm, Table, and Zeus information.

1. **Realms**

   - **-i** : display a realm detail.

**Example:**

```
list realms;
list realms -i;
```

![](https://blendata.atlassian.net/wiki/download/attachments/2255324015/image-20230330-055138.png?api=v2)

2. **Zeuses**

   - **-i** : display a zeus detail.

**Example:**

```
list zeuses;
list zeuses -i;
```

![](https://blendata.atlassian.net/wiki/download/attachments/2255324015/image-20230330-055201.png?api=v2)

3. **Tables**

   - **-i** : display a table detail.
   - **-n** : filter tables by table name.

**Example:**

```
list tables;
list tables -n "agg_%" -i;	
list tables -n "%spark%";
```

![](https://blendata.atlassian.net/wiki/download/attachments/2255324015/image-20230330-055240.png?api=v2)

![](https://blendata.atlassian.net/wiki/images/icons/grey_arrow_down.png)Query Command

This command uses to do data query using SQL.

- **-q** : query statement
- **-qf** : query file location
- **-f** : display format (support only CSV and JSON file)
- **-d** : separator for csv format (default is comma(,))

**Example:**

```
query -q “select * from covid_test limit 2”;
query -q “select * from covid_test limit 2” -f csv;
query -q “select * from covid_test limit 2” -f csv -d “|”;
query -qf "/statement/query.sql";
```

![](https://blendata.atlassian.net/wiki/download/attachments/2255324015/image-20230330-055420.png?api=v2)

![](https://blendata.atlassian.net/wiki/images/icons/grey_arrow_down.png)Export

This command uses to export the data as CSV or JSON file.

- **-q** : query statement
- **-f** : output format
- **-d** : separator for CSV format (default is comma(,))
- **-o** : output (the export file is always in the zip format)
- **-b** : print header (by default, it is set as false. If it is true, output will display column.)

**Example:**

```
export -q “select * from covid_test” -o “/tmp/store/custermer.zip”;
export -q “select * from covid_test” -d "|" -o “/tmp/store/custermer.zip”;
export -q "select * from profiles" -b true -o "/tmp/report/profile-report.zip";
```

![](https://blendata.atlassian.net/wiki/download/attachments/2255324015/image-20230330-081035.png?api=v2)![](https://blendata.atlassian.net/wiki/download/attachments/2255324015/image-20230330-055502.png?api=v2)![](https://blendata.atlassian.net/wiki/download/attachments/2255324015/image-20230330-055520.png?api=v2)

![](https://blendata.atlassian.net/wiki/images/icons/grey_arrow_down.png)Refresh Command

This command uses to refresh spark-table data.

**Example:**

```
refresh pong_no_spark_option;
```

![](https://blendata.atlassian.net/wiki/download/attachments/2255324015/image-20230330-055312.png?api=v2)

![](https://blendata.atlassian.net/wiki/images/icons/grey_arrow_down.png)Recovery Command

This command uses to recovery partitions of spark-table.

**Example:**

```
recovery customers;
```

![](https://blendata.atlassian.net/wiki/download/attachments/2255324015/image-20230330-055349.png?api=v2)

![](https://blendata.atlassian.net/wiki/images/icons/grey_arrow_down.png)Exit Command

This command uses to exit from system.

**Example:**

```
exit;
```

![](https://blendata.atlassian.net/wiki/download/attachments/2255324015/image-20230330-062827.png?api=v2)

![](https://blendata.atlassian.net/wiki/images/icons/grey_arrow_down.png)Non-Access Execute (Execute Immediately)

This command uses to execute the command without login.

- **-u** : user name
- **-p** : password
- **-pf** : password file location
- **-H** : HERA ip address
- **-P** : HERA port

```
bld-cli -u  <user name> -pf <password file location> -H <host of Hera url> -P <port> [-t <tenant name>] [-r <realm name>] [-z <zeus name>];
```

**Example:**

```
bld-cli -u John -pf <password file location> -H “heradomain.com” -P 8080;
bld-cli list tables -i -u "super_admin" -pf "/secret/my-cred.txt" -H "heradomain.com";
```

![](https://blendata.atlassian.net/wiki/download/attachments/2255324015/image-20230330-062539.png?api=v2)![](https://blendata.atlassian.net/wiki/download/attachments/2255324015/image-20230330-062635.png?api=v2)

![](https://blendata.atlassian.net/wiki/images/icons/grey_arrow_down.png)Generate Password File Command

This command uses to generate the password file. The password is stored as encrypted code.

- **-p** : password
- **-o** : output (password file location)

**Example:**

```
genPassFile -p "password" -o "password file location path";
```

![genpassfile.png](https://blendata.atlassian.net/wiki/download/attachments/2255324015/genpassfile.png?api=v2)

# Other utilities

![](https://blendata.atlassian.net/wiki/images/icons/grey_arrow_down.png)GenKey Command

This command uses to generate key for using decrypt in notebook application.

**Example:**

```
genkey "/Users/John/MyWork/tmp/key.csr" -s "Key-Salt";
```

![](https://blendata.atlassian.net/wiki/download/attachments/2255324015/image-20230330-055704.png?api=v2)

![](https://blendata.atlassian.net/wiki/images/icons/grey_arrow_down.png)getHardwareID Command

This command is used to get the hardware ID of a device for license creation or installation. (Users need the jar file before running this command.)

- **blendata-cli.jar**: fill in a jar file name.

**Example:**

```
java -jar <blendata-cli.jar> bld-cli public getHardwareID;
```

![image-20250901-025959.png](https://blendata.atlassian.net/wiki/download/attachments/2255324015/image-20250901-025959.png?api=v2)

## Guide Product Version Upgrade

| Field | Value |
|---|---|
| **URL** | https://blendata.atlassian.net/wiki/spaces/bde460/pages/2255324167/Guide+Product+Version+Upgrade |
| **Last modified** | 2026-06-17T10:37:39.989Z |
| **Breadcrumb** | Blendata Enterprise V.4.6.0 > Administrator User Manual > Related Documents |

[data-colorid=noyb03r3dg]{color:#bf2600} html[data-color-mode=dark] [data-colorid=noyb03r3dg]{color:#ff6640}To understand Blendata enterprise platform product upgrade. There are three types of upgrades: **major**, **minor**, and **patch.** You can see the definition of product version, go to [Product Version Definition](https://blendata.atlassian.net/wiki/spaces/bde460/pages/2255323266/Product+Version+Definition) page.

## **Major Upgrades**

Major upgrades is changed the version from Blendata enterprise platform module version 3.0.0 to 4.0.0 or higher.

A major upgrade typically has the following characteristics are:

- Large changes to functionality and update of core spark version.
- Extend other modules to the enterprise platform.
- Significant downtime is required to upgrade the cluster.

## **Minor Upgrades**

Minor upgrades upgrade your software to a higher minor version of a major release—for example from version 4.1.0 to version 4.2.0 —and typically include the following:

- New functionality
- Bug fixes
- Add on high priority request functionality.
- Downtime is optional to the major upgrade.

## **Patch Upgrades**

Patches fix critical bugs or address security issues. The version numbers for maintenance releases differ only in the fourth digit, for example, when upgrading from version 4.1.0 to 4.1.1

**\*\*Important\*\*** Blendata enterprise platform recommend that test upgrade on non-production environment before production.

---

# **Supported Upgrade**

The table below details the versions supported upgrade path of Blendata enterprise platform version.

|  |  |
| --- | --- |
| **Upgrade support from version** | **Upgrade to** |
| BDE 4.1.2 BDE 4.2.x  BDE 4.3.x  BDE 4.4.x  BDE 4.5.x  BDE 4.6.x | BDE 4.6.11 (Current Release Version) |

**Note:** To download BDE software need to contact our support team

Email: [support@blendata.co](mailto:support@blendata.co) (The Blendata Enterprise download link is valid for one week.)

## **Requirements and supported versions**

Hardware and Operating system requirements: see more on [Require Hardware](https://blendata.atlassian.net/wiki/spaces/BE1/pages/1895432810/Require+Hardware) page.
