## Run Service Actions Details

| Field | Value |
|---|---|
| **URL** | https://blendata.atlassian.net/wiki/spaces/bde460/pages/2727182340/Run+Service+Actions+Details |
| **Last modified** | 2025-11-19T04:36:36.674Z |
| **Breadcrumb** | Blendata Enterprise V.4.6.0 > Data Policy & Service Management > Services > Alert Scheduler |

This service provides two type of Run Service Actions, Kafka and SSH Run Script.

![image-20251119-033826.png](https://blendata.atlassian.net/wiki/download/attachments/2727182340/image-20251119-033826.png?api=v2)

---

## 1. Kafka Service

This service is used to send messages to a Kafka topic.  
All parameters are passed through `inbound.params` in JSON format, following the structure below.

```
{
  "target": {
    "receiveTopics": "string (required)",
    "receiveBrokers": "string (required)",
    "securityProtocol": "string (optional)",
    "truststoreLocation": "string (optional, required if securityProtocol=SSL)",
    "truststorePassword": "string (optional, required if securityProtocol=SSL)",
    "keystoreLocation": "string (optional, required if securityProtocol=SSL)",
    "keystorePassword": "string (optional, required if securityProtocol=SSL)",
    "keyPassword": "string (optional, required if securityProtocol=SSL)"
  }
}
```

### Parameter Details

- Required Parameters

| Parameter | Type | Description |
| --- | --- | --- |
| `receiveTopics` | String | Name of the Kafka topic that you want to send messages to. |
| `receiveBrokers` | String | Kafka broker addresses (e.g., `"localhost:9092"` or `"broker1:9092,broker2:9092"`). |

- SSL Parameters (required only if SSL is used - Optional)

| Parameter | Type | Description |
| --- | --- | --- |
| `securityProtocol` | String | Security protocol (must be `"SSL"` when SSL is required). |
| `truststoreLocation` | String | Path to the truststore file (required when `securityProtocol = "SSL"`). |
| `truststorePassword` | String | Password for the truststore (required when `securityProtocol = "SSL"`). |
| `keystoreLocation` | String | Path to the keystore file (required when `securityProtocol = "SSL"`). |
| `keystorePassword` | String | Password for the keystore (required when `securityProtocol = "SSL"`). |
| `keyPassword` | String | Password for the private key (required when `securityProtocol = "SSL"`). |

If `securityProtocol` is not specified or is not `"SSL"`, the connection will default to a non-SSL connection.

### Example 1: Non-SSL Connection

```
{
  "target": {
    "receiveTopics": "notification-topic",
    "receiveBrokers": "localhost:9092"
  }
}
```

### Example 2: SSL Connection

```
{
  "target": {
    "receiveTopics": "secure-notification-topic",
    "receiveBrokers": "kafka-broker:9093",
    "securityProtocol": "SSL",
    "truststoreLocation": "/path/to/truststore.jks",
    "truststorePassword": "truststore-password",
    "keystoreLocation": "/path/to/keystore.jks",
    "keystorePassword": "keystore-password",
    "keyPassword": "key-password"
  }
}
```

---

## 2. SSH Run Script Service

This service is used to run a script on a remote server via an SSH connection.  
All parameters are passed through `inbound.params` in JSON format, following the structure below.

This service requires SSH key-based authentication, using a private key located at `~/.ssh/id_rsa`. Click here to learn [**how to generate the SSH keypair and set permissions on the remote server.**](https://blendata.atlassian.net/wiki/spaces/BEV/pages/2727182340/Run+Service+Actions+Details#How-to-generate-Keypair-and-Set-permission-with-Remote-server)

```
{
  "target": {
    "userSSH": "string (required)",
    "ipHost": "string (required)",
    "port": "string (required)",
    "pathFileName": "string (required)"
  }
}
```

### Parameter Details

| Parameter | Type | Description |
| --- | --- | --- |
| `userSSH` | String | Username for the SSH connection |
| `ipHost` | String | IP address or hostname of the remote server |
| `port` | String | Port number for SSH connection (will be converted to Integer) |
| `pathFileName` | String | Path to the script file that will be executed on the remote server |

- The script will be executed using the command: `bash "<pathFileName>"`
- If the script runs successfully, the service will return the output from **stdout**.
- If the script encounters an error, the service will throw an exception along with the error message returned from **stderr**.

### Example: SSH Run Script Connection

```
{
  "target": {
    "userSSH": "admin",
    "ipHost": "192.168.1.100",
    "port": "22",
    "pathFileName": "/home/admin/scripts/backup.sh"
  }
}
```

- Ensure that the private key (`~/.ssh/id_rsa`) has permission to access the remote server.
- The script file on the remote server must have execute permission.

---

## How to generate Keypair and Set permission with Remote server

[SSH Run Script Connection](https://blendata.atlassian.net/wiki/spaces/BEV/pages/2727182340/Run+Service+Actions+Details#2.-SSH-Run-Script-Service) requires an SSH keypair, follow the steps below to generate the keypair and configure permissions.

### 1. Generate Keypair from Noti-Serv serve**r**

```
# gen keypair for self ssh with Noti-serv
ssh-keygen
...
Enter passphrase (empty for no passphrase):
Enter same passphrase again:
Your identification has been saved in /home/ec2-user/.ssh/id_rsa.
Your public key has been saved in /home/ec2-user/.ssh/id_rsa.pub.
...
|      . Bo**o+o..|
|       o+*+=Bo.. |
+----[SHA256]-----+
# ls ~/.ssh/
id_rsa  id_rsa.pub
# authorized generated public key from Noti-serv
cat ~/.ssh/id_rsa.pub
```

### 2. Setup public key to Remote server

To support remote from Noti-Serv server.

```
# copy text from id_rsa.pub in Noti-servNoti-servNoti-serv 
# to add in ~/.ssh/authorized_keys at target remoted server
vi ~/.ssh/authorized_keys
```

---

## Connecting Python to Blendata Enterprise via Hive JDBC

| Field | Value |
|---|---|
| **URL** | https://blendata.atlassian.net/wiki/spaces/bde460/pages/2735538178/Connecting+Python+to+Blendata+Enterprise+via+Hive+JDBC |
| **Last modified** | 2025-11-25T12:02:24.993Z |
| **Breadcrumb** | Blendata Enterprise V.4.6.0 > Integration > Connect to Blendata with JDBC/ODBC |

## (blue star) Pre-requirement

1. Download the hive-jdbc-2.3.10-standalone.jar file from the [link](https://repo1.maven.org/maven2/org/apache/hive/hive-jdbc/2.3.10/).
2. Save the hive-jdbc-2.3.10-standalone.jar to any directory and remember its file path, as you will need to reference this path when using it in Python.
3. Open Terminal, Shell, CMD, or any service that can run Python.
4. Install the required Python packages by running the command:

   ```
   pip install jaydebeapi jpype1
   ```
5. Verify the installation of jaydebeapi and jpype1 (for example, in Bash/CMD):

   ```
   python3
   ```
6. In the Python prompt, run the following commands, if the installation is correct, it will print “OK”.

   ```
   import jaydebeapi
   import jpype
   print("OK")
   ```

## (blue star) How to get data with python via Hive JDBC

1. Open a Terminal, Shell, CMD, Jupyter Notebook, VS Code Terminal, or any environment that can run Python.
2. Use the following sample code and replace the connection information (host, port, user, password, database, and path to the JDBC .jar file):

   1. Full Configuration Example (Explicit Parameters)

      ```
      import jaydebeapi

      hive_jdbc_driver = "org.apache.hive.jdbc.HiveDriver"
      jdbc_url = (
          "jdbc:hive2://your-hive-host:10000/default;"
          "transportMode=binary;"
          "hive.server2.thrift.http.path=cliservice"
      )

      conn = jaydebeapi.connect(
          hive_jdbc_driver,
          jdbc_url,
          {"user": "your_user", "password": "your_password"},
          "/path/to/hive-jdbc-2.3.10-standalone.jar"
      )

      cursor = conn.cursor()
      cursor.execute("SELECT current_database()")
      print(cursor.fetchall())
      ```
   2. Minimal Configuration Example (Simplified Version)

      ```
      import jaydebeapi

      hive_driver_path = "/opt/hive_jdbc/hive-jdbc-2.3.10-standalone.jar"

      conn = jaydebeapi.connect(
          "org.apache.hive.jdbc.HiveDriver",
          "jdbc:hive2://your-host:10000/default",
          {"user": "youruser", "password": "yourpass"},
          hive_driver_path
      )

      cursor = conn.cursor()
      cursor.execute("SELECT current_database()")
      print(cursor.fetchall())
      ```

## (blue star) Explanation of key parameters

- **import jaydebeapi:** imports the library required for creating JDBC connections from Python.
- **hive\_jdbc\_driver:** fixed value → `"org.apache.hive.jdbc.HiveDriver"`.
- **jdbc\_url:** JDBC connection string that defines the Hive host, port, database, and connection options. *e.g.,* `jdbc:hive2://your-host:your-port/database_name`
- **conn:** this object represents an active JDBC session to the Hive server, maintains the communication channel, and requires authentication.
- **cursor:** cursor object for executing SQL queries and fetching results.

---

## How to Change tmp Path (for noexec /tmp)

| Field | Value |
|---|---|
| **URL** | https://blendata.atlassian.net/wiki/spaces/bde460/pages/2759098369/How+to+Change+tmp+Path+for+noexec+tmp |
| **Last modified** | 2025-11-27T11:20:23.045Z |
| **Breadcrumb** | Blendata Enterprise V.4.6.0 > Administrator User Manual |

In case the security team on customer site currently does not permit the removal of the **noexec** option from `/tmp` in the **/etc/fstab** file. This restriction is blocking the Zeus service from starting, as the service requires `/tmp` to be mounted with the **exec** option.

We resolved this issue by simply adjusting the Zeus path configuration to use a local directory instead of the default `/tmp` location.

1. Go to **start-zeus.sh** file.

```
vi <zeus_name>/bin/start-zeus.sh
```

2. Edit the path configuration below.

   - `spark.executor.extraJavaOptions`= **-Djava.io.tmpdir=/path/to/local/tmp**
   - `spark.driver.extraJavaOptions`=**-Djava.io.tmpdir=/path/to/local/tmp**

```
...

  --conf "spark.executor.extraJavaOptions=-Djava.io.tmpdir=/path/to/local/tmp -Dsedona.global.charset=utf8 -XX:+UseG1GC -XX:+UseCompressedOops -XX:+PrintGCDetails -XX:+PrintGCDateStamps -XX:InitiatingHeapOccupancyPercent=35 -XX:G1HeapRegionSize=${EXECUTOR_HEAP_REGION_SIZE} -XX:+AlwaysPreTouch -Xloggc:${ZEUS_LOG}/gcexe.log" \

  --conf "spark.driver.extraJavaOptions=-Djava.io.tmpdir=/path/to/local/tmp -Dsedona.global.charset=utf8 -Dspring.config.location=${SPRING_CONFIG_LOCATION} -DZEUS_LOG=${ZEUS_LOG} -XX:+UseG1GC -XX:+UseCompressedOops -XX:+PrintGCDetails -XX:+PrintGCDateStamps -XX:InitiatingHeapOccupancyPercent=35 -XX:G1HeapRegionSize=${DRIVER_HEAP_REGION_SIZE} -XX:+AlwaysPreTouch -Xloggc:${ZEUS_LOG}/gcDriverlog.log" \

...
```

3. Restart the Zeus using below commands.

```
cd <zeus_name>
bin/stop-zeus.sh
bin/checkPIDStatus.sh
bin/start-zeus.sh
```

---

## Blendata Service Port Mapping

| Field | Value |
|---|---|
| **URL** | https://blendata.atlassian.net/wiki/spaces/bde460/pages/2770272258/Blendata+Service+Port+Mapping |
| **Last modified** | 2026-06-04T08:24:52.296Z |
| **Breadcrumb** | Blendata Enterprise V.4.6.0 > General References |

If you have accessed the Blendata platform and used the SQL Editor to run queries and view results, you may have wondered how the backend modules actually communicate behind the platform.

This page outlines the services running within each Blendata module and explains how these services interact and communicate with one another through designated network ports.

Before proceeding, it is recommended to review the [Architecture Component](https://blendata.atlassian.net/wiki/x/JIBthg) Overview and understand [the basics](https://blendata.atlassian.net/wiki/x/AoBifg) of how each module operates within the Blendata platform UI.

## Port Mapping Diagram

### Module Communication Overview:

This diagram illustrates the overall communication flow between major Blendata modules and how they work together as a unified platform before diving into service-level details.

![BDE_Standard_Architect-Page-18.drawio-20251212-045052.png](https://blendata.atlassian.net/wiki/download/attachments/2770272258/BDE_Standard_Architect-Page-18.drawio-20251212-045052.png?api=v2)

| **Module** | **Services** |
| --- | --- |
| Web Module | Hera  Cerberus  Notification  BDE Management Agent (Genesis) |
| Notebook Module | Zeppelin web Zeppelin python interpreater Zeppelin java interpreater BDE Management Agent (Genesis) |
| Workflow Module | Workflow Agent Workflow Web (Apache Airflow) Workflow Scheduler Workflow Worker BDE Management Agent (Genesis) |
| HPC Module | Zues |
| Streaming Module | Kafka Debezium |
| Backend Module | Redis Database metastore (PostgreSQL) Spark master Spark history Zookeeper (for HA spark master) BDE Management Agent (Genesis) |
| BDM | Blendata management |

### Service Communication Overview Across Modules:

![BDE_Standard_Architect-DOC Draft1.drawio (1)-20251215-031409.png](https://blendata.atlassian.net/wiki/download/attachments/2770272258/BDE_Standard_Architect-DOC%20Draft1.drawio%20(1)-20251215-031409.png?api=v2)

| **No.** | **Module to Module** | **Source** | **Destination** | **Port** | **Description** |
| --- | --- | --- | --- | --- | --- |
| **User Connect to Web Browser UI** | | | | | |
| 1 | User➜ Web Browser ➜ Web Module | Eros | Hera | 11010 | BDE Web Ul default url: https://<ip>:11010 |
| 2 | User ➜ Web Browser ➜ Notebook Module | Web Browser | Zeppelin web | 8998 | BDE Notebook Ul default url: https://<ip>:8998 |
| **User Connect to Web Browser Monitor** | | | | | |
| 4 | User ➜ Web Browser ➜ Backend Module | Web Browser | Spark History | 18080 | For monitor history of spark application that stopped. |
| 5 | User ➜ Web Browser ➜ HPC Module | Web Browser | Zeus | 4040 - 4045 | For monitor spark job (Task job in BDE)Remark: The number of running port by number of zues service. |
| 6 | User ➜ Web Browser ➜ BDM | Web Browser | BDM | 8000 | BDM Ul default url: https://<ip>:8000 |
| **Web Module** | | | | | |
| 7 | Web Browser ➜ Web Module | Eros | Hera | 11010 |  |
| 8 | Web Module ➜ Web Module | Hera | Notification | 10008 | Manage notification service in BDE. |
| 9 | Web Module ➜ Web Module | Notification | Hera | 11010 |  |
| 10 | Web Module ➜ Web Module | Hera | Cerberus | 11020 |  |
| 11 | Web Module ➜ Web Module | Cerberus | Notification | 10008 |  |
| 12 | Web Module ➜ Backend Module | Notification | Metadata | 5432 | Database PostgreSQL, if using mySQL the port is 3306. |
| 13 | Web Module ➜ Backend Module | Hera | Redis | 6379 | In-Memory Cache & Session Store Sentinel TCP (Port 26379) authen node in redis. |
| 14 | Web Module ➜ Backend Module | Cerberus | Redis | 6379 | In-Memory Cache & Session Store Sentinel TCP (Port 26379) authen node in redis. |
| 15 | Web Module ➜ Backend Module | Hera | Metadata | 5432 | Database PostgreSQL, if using mySQL the port is 3306. |
| 16 | Web Module ➜ Backend Module | Cerberus | Metadata | 5432 | Database PostgreSQL, if using mySQL the port is 3306. |
| 17 | Web Module ➜ Notebook Module | Hera | Zeppelin web | 8998 | BDE Notebook Ul default url: https://<ip>:8998 |
| 18 | Web Module ➜ Workflow Module | Hera | Workflow Agent | 12020 |  |
| 19 | Web Module ➜ HPC Module | Hera | Zeus | 11000 - 11005 | Call to tricker/command process Remark: The number of running port by number of zues service |
| 20 | Web Module ➜ Streaming Module | Hera | Kafka | 9092 |  |
| **Notebook Module** | | | | | |
| 21 | Notebook Module ➜ Web Module | Zeppelin web | Hera | 11010 |  |
| 22 | Notebook Module ➜ Web Module | Zeppelin web | Cerberus | 11020 |  |
| 23 | Notebook Module ➜ Streaming Module | Zeppelin web | Kafka | 9092 |  |
| 24 | Notebook Module ➜ Backend Module | Zeppelin web | Spark Master | 7077 |  |
| 25 | Notebook Module ➜ Backend Module | Zeppelin web | Metadata | 5432 | Database PostgreSQL, if using mySQL the port is 3306. |
| **Workflow Module** | | | | | |
| 26 | Workflow Module ➜ Web Module | Workflow Worker | Hera | 11010 |  |
| 27 | Workflow Module ➜ Notebook Module | Workflow Worker | Zeppelin web | 8998 |  |
| 28 | Workflow Module ➜ Backend Module | Workflow Agent | Metadata | 5432 | Database PostgreSQL, if using mySQL the port is 3306. |
| **HPC Module** | | | | | |
| 29 | HPC Module ➜ Web Module | Zeus | Cerberus | 11020 |  |
| 30 | HPC Module ➜ Web Module | Zeus | Notification | 10008 | Manage notification service in BDE. |
| 31 | HPC Module ➜ Backend Module | Zeus | Metadata | 5432 | Database PostgreSQL, if using mySQL the port is 3306. |
| 32 | HPC Module ➜ Backend Module | Zeus | Spark Master | 7077 |  |
| **Backend Module** | | | | | |
| 33 | Backend Module ➜ Backend Module | Spark Master | Zookeeper | 2181 | Manage and synchronized status of kafka broker. |
| 34 | Backend Module ➜ Streaming Module | Spark Master | Kafka | 9092 |  |
| 35 | Backend Module ➜ Backend Module | Spark Master | Metadata | 5432 | Database PostgreSQL, if using mySQL the port is 3306. |
| **Blendata Management** | | | | | |
| 36 | BDE Web module (BDM agent),  BDE Backend module (BDM agent),  BDE HPC module (BDM agent),  BDE Worker module (BDM agent),  BDE Notebook module (BDM agent),  BDE Workflow module (BDM agent). | Blendata Management | BDE Management Agent | 12001 |  |
| 37 | Blendata Management | Blendata Management | Web Monitor | 8000 |  |
| **Connect JDBC/ODBC** | | | | | |
| 38 | BI service ➜ HPC Module | Connect JDBC/ODBC | HPC Application server (Zeus-Thrift Port) | 9995- 9999 |  |

If multiple instances are running and a **load balancer** is required:

- Hera and Cerberus: use port 443 (HTTPS) through the load balancer.
- Metadata database:

  - PostgreSQL: port 5432
  - MySQL: port 3306

## How to Setup RSA / AES Encryption UDF for Compute Module (ZEUS)

| Field | Value |
|---|---|
| **URL** | https://blendata.atlassian.net/wiki/spaces/bde460/pages/2867200002/How+to+Setup+RSA+AES+Encryption+UDF+for+Compute+Module+ZEUS |
| **Last modified** | 2026-01-12T08:59:57.222Z |
| **Breadcrumb** | Blendata Enterprise V.4.6.0 > Administrator User Manual |

# Objective

Configure **RSA / AES Encryption User-Defined Functions (UDFs)** for **BDE Compute Module (ZEUS)**  
to support data encryption and decryption via Spark SQL.

## 1. Required File Structure

All encryption-related files must be placed under:

```
/home/{blendata-user}/.bde/udf-conf
├── bde-aes.key
├── bde-secure.conf
└── bde-secure.jks
```

- `{blendata-user}` is the Linux user running ZEUS / Spark.

### File Descriptions

| File | Description |
| --- | --- |
| `bde-aes.key` | Stores the **AES secret key** used for symmetric encryption and decryption of data. This key is used by AES-based UDFs (`aes_enc`, `aes_dec`). |
| `bde-secure.conf` | Main configuration file for encryption UDFs. Defines RSA keystore credentials, key alias, and private key password required for RSA operations. |
| `bde-secure.jks` | Java KeyStore (JKS) containing the **RSA key pair** (public/private keys). The public key is used for encryption, and the private key is used for decryption and key protection. |

## 2. Required Configuration

### 2.1 RSA Configuration (`bde-secure.conf`)

Example:

```
bde.enc.rsa.keystore.pass=P@ssw0rd
bde.enc.rsa.key.alias=BDE_ENC
bde.enc.rsa.key.pass=P@ssw0rd
```

- `keystore.pass` : JKS password.
- `key.alias` : RSA key alias.
- `key.pass` : RSA private key password.

### 2.2 AES Key (`bde-aes.key`)

- Stores the AES secret key.
- Must be managed as an external file (not embedded in code).

Recommended permissions:

```
chmod 600 bde-aes.key bde-secure.conf bde-secure.jks
```

## 3. Post-Configuration Steps

After placing all files:

- Restart ZEUS.
- Verify logs to ensure Encryption UDFs are successfully loaded.

## 4. Usage Example (Spark SQL)

```
SELECT
    rsa_enc(name)                  AS rsa_encrypt,
    rsa_dec(rsa_enc(name))         AS rsa_decrypt,
    aes_enc(name)                  AS aes_encrypt,
    aes_dec(aes_enc(name))         AS aes_decrypt
FROM basic_p
LIMIT 10;
```

Expected result:

- `*_decrypt` values must match the original `product` value.
- Encrypted columns should be unreadable ciphertext.

Example result:

![image-20260112-081008.png](https://blendata.atlassian.net/wiki/download/attachments/2867200002/image-20260112-081008.png?api=v2)

---

## How to Disable the Default Schema Restriction Feature in Blendata v4.6.12+

| Field | Value |
|---|---|
| **URL** | https://blendata.atlassian.net/wiki/spaces/bde460/pages/2869100546/How+to+Disable+the+Default+Schema+Restriction+Feature+in+Blendata+v4.6.12 |
| **Last modified** | 2026-01-13T03:42:18.789Z |
| **Breadcrumb** | Blendata Enterprise V.4.6.0 > Administrator User Manual > Related Documents |

In Blendata v4.6.12 and above, the default schema can be accessed only by Super Admin users.  
If you upgrade from any earlier version to v4.6.12, you may find that the default schema is no longer accessible to general users.

This behavior is controlled by a configuration setting and can be disabled if required.

## How to Disable the Default Schema Restriction

1. Go to the Hera configuration directory.
2. Open the following file.

   ```
   config/application.properties
   ```
3. Add or update the following configuration.

   ```
   #Turn on/off permission check for default schema
   #on = true, off = false

   permission.default-schema.enabled=false
   ```

Setting `permission.default-schema.enabled=false` will allow general users to access the default schema.

---

## Parquet Bloom Filter Setting

| Field | Value |
|---|---|
| **URL** | https://blendata.atlassian.net/wiki/spaces/bde460/pages/2923626499/Parquet+Bloom+Filter+Setting |
| **Last modified** | 2026-02-02T05:18:19.974Z |
| **Breadcrumb** | Blendata Enterprise V.4.6.0 > General References |

A Bloom filter is a space-saving data structure used to check if an item belongs to a set. It is designed for high speed and responds to queries with either '**definitely no**' or '**probably yes**.' While it never has false negatives, the probability of false positives is determined when the filter is first initialized.

Users are able to setup this bloom filter during data ingestion process via **Ingest Advanced options** menu in Setting Output Options page.

1. Import your data into the Blendata Enteprise platform.
2. During output option setting, click “**Ingest Advanced options**” option.

![Ingest Advanced options.png](https://blendata.atlassian.net/wiki/download/attachments/2923626499/Ingest%20Advanced%20options.png?api=v2)

3. Set up the Bloom filter by adding the configuration in the **Advanced Sink Configuration** section

![ingest advanced option.png](https://blendata.atlassian.net/wiki/download/attachments/2923626499/ingest%20advanced%20option.png?api=v2)

4. Add the bloom filter parameters and values.

![spark sink conf.png](https://blendata.atlassian.net/wiki/download/attachments/2923626499/spark%20sink%20conf.png?api=v2)

- `parquet.bloom.filter.enabled`: To enable writing bloom filter. If it is true, the bloom filter will be enable for all columns. It is also possible to enable it for some columns by specifying the column name within the property followed by #.

```
// Enable the bloom filter for all columns
conf.set("parquet.bloom.filter.enabled", true);
// Eable the bloom filter for the column 'column.path'
conf.set("parquet.bloom.filter.enabled#column.path", true);
// Disable the bloom filter for the column 'column.path'
conf.set("parquet.bloom.filter.enabled#column.path", false);
```

- `parquet.bloom.filter.expected.ndv`: The expected number of distinct values in a column, it is used to compute the optimal size of the bloom filter such as 100000.

If this parameter is not set, the bloom filter will use the maximum size.

```
// The bloom filter will be enabled for 'column.path' with expected number of distinct values equals to 200
conf.set("parquet.bloom.filter.expected.ndv#column.path", 200)
```

- `parquet.bloom.filter.fpp`: The maximum expected false positive probability for the bloom filter in a column. (If this parameter is not set, it defaults to 0.01.)

```
// The bloom filter will be enabled for 'column.path' with expected number of distinct values equals to 200 and maximum expected false positive probability sets to 0.02
conf.set("parquet.bloom.filter.expected.ndv#column.path", 200)
conf.set("parquet.bloom.filter.fpp#column.path", 0.02)
```

- `parquet.bloom.filter.max.bytes`: The maximum number of bytes for a bloom filter bitset. Default value: 1048576 (1MB)

5. Save your configuration and proceed with the import steps as usual.

---

## Workflow Execution Examples

| Field | Value |
|---|---|
| **URL** | https://blendata.atlassian.net/wiki/spaces/bde460/pages/2944368677/Workflow+Execution+Examples |
| **Last modified** | 2026-02-23T11:47:17.791Z |
| **Breadcrumb** | Blendata Enterprise V.4.6.0 > Workflow Management |

## Overview

This page provides examples of workflow execution to help users understand how task dependencies and execution conditions affect workflow behavior.

Before starting the execution, you need to create tasks by following these simple steps:

1. You can create tasks to execute jobs using the services provided in the UI.
2. For each task, you can configure options such as Retries, Run After conditions, Forward Result, Timeout, etc.
3. After configuring the tasks, deploy the workflow and execute it for testing.

### Example 1

In this workflow, tasks are executed based on the following conditional dependencies.

- **Task 2**: Runs if **Task 1** completes successfully; otherwise, it is skipped.

![image-20260208-143200.png](https://blendata.atlassian.net/wiki/download/attachments/2944368677/image-20260208-143200.png?api=v2)

Execution Results Based on Conditions.

- Case 1: Task 1 Succeeds.  
  When Task 1 completes successfully, Task 2 is executed as expected.

![image-20260208-143906.png](https://blendata.atlassian.net/wiki/download/attachments/2944368677/image-20260208-143906.png?api=v2)

- Case 2: Task 1 Fails.  
  When Task 1 fails, Task 2 is not executed and receives an UPSTREAM\_FAILED status.

![image-20260208-144432.png](https://blendata.atlassian.net/wiki/download/attachments/2944368677/image-20260208-144432.png?api=v2)

---

### Example 2

In this workflow, tasks are executed based on the following conditional dependencies.

- **Task 2**: Runs if **Task 1** completes failed; otherwise, it is skipped.

![image-20260208-145140.png](https://blendata.atlassian.net/wiki/download/attachments/2944368677/image-20260208-145140.png?api=v2)

Execution Results Based on Conditions.

- Case 1: Task 1 Succeeds.  
  When Task 1 completes successfully, Task 2 is not executed and receives a SKIPPED status.

![image-20260208-144948.png](https://blendata.atlassian.net/wiki/download/attachments/2944368677/image-20260208-144948.png?api=v2)

- Case 2: Task 1 Fails.  
  When Task 1 fails, Task 2 is executed as expected.

![image-20260208-145432.png](https://blendata.atlassian.net/wiki/download/attachments/2944368677/image-20260208-145432.png?api=v2)

---

### Example 3

In this workflow, tasks are executed based on the following conditional dependencies.

- **Task 2**: Runs if **Task 1** completes successfully; otherwise, it is skipped.
- **Task 3**: Runs if **Task 2** completes successfully.
- **Task 4**: Runs if **Task 2** fails.
- **Task 5**: Runs after **Task 3** completes successfully.

![image-20260208-141325.png](https://blendata.atlassian.net/wiki/download/attachments/2944368677/image-20260208-141325.png?api=v2)

Execution Results Based on Conditions.

- Case 1: Task 1 Succeeds.

![image-20260208-150031.png](https://blendata.atlassian.net/wiki/download/attachments/2944368677/image-20260208-150031.png?api=v2)

- Case 2: Task 2 Fails.

![image-20260209-070803.png](https://blendata.atlassian.net/wiki/download/attachments/2944368677/image-20260209-070803.png?api=v2)

- Case 3: Task 1 Fails.

![image-20260223-114700.png](https://blendata.atlassian.net/wiki/download/attachments/2944368677/image-20260223-114700.png?api=v2)

---

## Git Workspace (V4.6.13)

| Field | Value |
|---|---|
| **URL** | https://blendata.atlassian.net/wiki/spaces/bde460/pages/2965405797/Git+Workspace+V4.6.13 |
| **Last modified** | 2026-02-24T04:30:41.098Z |
| **Breadcrumb** | Blendata Enterprise V.4.6.0 > Explore and Process Data > Notebook |

![notebook v4.6.13.png](https://blendata.atlassian.net/wiki/download/attachments/2965405797/notebook%20v4.6.13.png?api=v2)

1. **Navigation path**: shows your current location.
2. **Actions menu**![image-20250828-152902.png](https://blendata.atlassian.net/wiki/download/attachments/2965405797/4dc39b06-4e1d-4da6-8bf8-ea06d5c7d845?api=v2): action for workspace.

   1. **Edit Workspace Details**: to edit description or tag for workspace.
   2. **Refresh Git Connection:** refresh the connection status with the Git repository.
   3. **Git Settings**: update Git credentials (username and token) when expired or changed.
   4. **Manage Permission**: to assign workspace permission to other users.
3. **Sync:** all new notes from the local notebook environment or Git (if available) will be automatically created here, with the sync executor set as the owner.
4. **Branch**: this is the branch location your current Git server.

   1. **Merge**: the branch comparison and conflict resolution feature allows users to identify changes and resolve conflicts directly within the workspace.
   2. **Checkout**: switch the notebook environment by changing the Git branch. This will overwrite all notebooks in the current local environment with those from the selected branch.
   3. **Create New Branch**: to create a new branch in your environment, but the new branch will duplicated from current branch.
   4. **Manage Git Branch**: to manage the Git branch and local branch by deleting the branch in this option
5. **Repo:** show Repository name in Git.
6. **Commit**: this is the commit information like commit ID and commit message.
7. **Changing page view**:view additional notebooks.
8. **+ Notebook**: you can perform two actions including create a new notebook and upload notebook.
9. **Actions**: allow users to perform additional actions like open a notebook, edit note details, push to git, export a notebook, and delete a notebook.
10. **Search**: find a notes.
11. **Only My Assets**: click to show only my notes.
12. **Sort by**: sort notebooks by Title, Creation Date or Last access.

---

# Sync Notebook Profile Action

![](https://blendata.atlassian.net/wiki/download/attachments/2965405797/git%20sync%20+%20action.png?api=v2)

## Sync

Sync option is used to update the Notebook profile between the local notebook environment and Git server. In case of a conflict where both the Git location and Zeppelin are updated with the same Notebook profile simultaneously, an error may occur, '**Hard Pull**' feature, which forces synchronization by overwriting Zeppelin with the latest data from Git.

![image-20250828-052219.png](https://blendata.atlassian.net/wiki/download/attachments/2965405797/image-20250828-052219.png?api=v2)

1. Click “**Sync**” option.
2. Fill in your current Git branch name.
3. (Optional) Enable “**Hard Pull Sync from Git**”.

   - If users select this option, a backup option will be display, which all Notebooks in your local environment will be backup on backend.

![image-20250828-052255.png](https://blendata.atlassian.net/wiki/download/attachments/2965405797/image-20250828-052255.png?api=v2)

If you need to restore a backup file, please contact the super administrator to restore the backup Notebook in your environment.

4. Click “**Sync**”button to update the Notebook profile.

---

# Branch Action

This is the branch location your current Git server.

![sync git profilt (v4.6.13).png](https://blendata.atlassian.net/wiki/download/attachments/2965405797/sync%20git%20profilt%20(v4.6.13).png?api=v2)

## Merge

This branch comparison and conflict resolution feature allows users to identify changes and resolve conflicts directly within the workspace. Users can reconcile differences and maintain code consistency before integration. Ultimately, this safeguards the main branch by preventing the accidental overwriting of critical changes and ensuring a stable, conflict-free merge process.

![image-20260223-105058.png](https://blendata.atlassian.net/wiki/download/attachments/2965405797/image-20260223-105058.png?api=v2)

1. Navigate to your target branch.
2. Select the “**Merge**” option and choose the branch you wish to merge into your current branch.
3. Click the **“Merge”** button. The platform will automatically compare the two branches. If changes are detected, a popup will appear with a **“View Changes”** button. If conflicts are found, the **Merge Changes** popup will display the conflict details as described in Step 4.

![image-20260223-105301.png](https://blendata.atlassian.net/wiki/download/attachments/2965405797/image-20260223-105301.png?api=v2)

- **Additions:** New notebooks from the selected branch will be identified.

![image-20260224-042510.png](https://blendata.atlassian.net/wiki/download/attachments/2965405797/image-20260224-042510.png?api=v2)

4. Review the change details if **conflicts are detected**.

- **Conflicts:** If a notebook has conflicts, the differences will be displayed. You can resolve these by editing the code or statements directly in the right panel.

![image-20260223-110119.png](https://blendata.atlassian.net/wiki/download/attachments/2965405797/image-20260223-110119.png?api=v2)

- **Additions:** New notebooks from the selected branch will be identified.

![image-20260223-110433.png](https://blendata.atlassian.net/wiki/download/attachments/2965405797/image-20260223-110433.png?api=v2)

- **Deletions:** Notebooks that have been deleted will be noted.

![image-20260223-110824.png](https://blendata.atlassian.net/wiki/download/attachments/2965405797/image-20260223-110824.png?api=v2)

5. Once you have resolved the conflicts for each notebook, check the **“Resolved”** box and click **“Commit Merge”** to finalize the process.

![image-20260223-111551.png](https://blendata.atlassian.net/wiki/download/attachments/2965405797/image-20260223-111551.png?api=v2)

## Checkout

This option is used to change the notebook environment by switching the Git branch. In case, users need to overwrite all Notebooks in the current local environment with selected branch, you can enable “**Hard Checkout**“ option.

![checkout.png](https://blendata.atlassian.net/wiki/download/attachments/2965405797/checkout.png?api=v2)

1. Select a branch.
2. (Optional) Enable the “**Hard Checkout**“ option.

   - If you select this option, you need to fill in the current local branch name.

![checkout with hard option.png](https://blendata.atlassian.net/wiki/download/attachments/2965405797/checkout%20with%20hard%20option.png?api=v2)

3. Click “**Checkout**“ button.

## Create New Branch

To create a new branch in your environment, but the new branch will duplicated from current branch.

![create new branch.png](https://blendata.atlassian.net/wiki/download/attachments/2965405797/create%20new%20branch.png?api=v2)

1. Fill in a new branch name.
2. Click “**Create New Branch**“.

## Manage Git Branch

Users are able to manage the Git branch and local branch by deleting the branch in this option.

![manage git branch.png](https://blendata.atlassian.net/wiki/download/attachments/2965405797/manage%20git%20branch.png?api=v2)

1. Click ![(error)](https://blendata.atlassian.net/wiki/s/-2069522349/6452/eaae94c6a2affaf35e508f35b397601c3535ccda/_/images/icons/emoticons/error.png) icon to delete the branch.
2. Enter your branch name to confirm.

![image-20250829-052107.png](https://blendata.atlassian.net/wiki/download/attachments/2965405797/image-20250829-052107.png?api=v2)

3. Click “**Delete**“ button.

## SSO User Profile Creation Request

| Field | Value |
|---|---|
| **URL** | https://blendata.atlassian.net/wiki/spaces/bde460/pages/2966880258/SSO+User+Profile+Creation+Request |
| **Last modified** | 2026-02-23T09:21:18.531Z |
| **Breadcrumb** | Blendata Enterprise V.4.6.0 > Integration |

To streamline the SSO user import process, new users can now automatically create their own profiles upon login. However, for security purposes, these profiles still require administrative approval before access is granted. This is available for Blendata Enterprise version 4.6.13 or newer.

![image-20260223-073805.png](https://blendata.atlassian.net/wiki/download/attachments/2966880258/image-20260223-073805.png?api=v2)

1. Go to the Blendata Enterprise website and click “**Create Account**” option.
2. Fill in the account information.

   - **Email**: Your email address.
   - **First Name**: Fill in your first name.
   - **Last Name**: Fill in your last name.
   - **SSO Source**: Select the SSO type including Microsoft AD FS or Azure.

![image-20260223-074756.png](https://blendata.atlassian.net/wiki/download/attachments/2966880258/image-20260223-074756.png?api=v2)

3. Your account information has been saved. Your request has been sent to the administrator and is currently awaiting approval. Once approved, you will receive an email notification.

![image-20260223-075054.png](https://blendata.atlassian.net/wiki/download/attachments/2966880258/image-20260223-075054.png?api=v2)

## Audit Log Setup and TimeScale (Offline Installation)

| Field | Value |
|---|---|
| **URL** | https://blendata.atlassian.net/wiki/spaces/bde460/pages/2977398792/Audit+Log+Setup+and+TimeScale+Offline+Installation |
| **Last modified** | 2026-03-25T03:45:25.248Z |
| **Breadcrumb** | Audit Log Installation |

To install the Audit Log service on a server with no internet access.

## Prerequisites

1. Perform the **PostgreSQL** package download on a server with internet access.

   1. <https://download.postgresql.org/pub/repos/yum/15/redhat/rhel-9.7-x86_64/>
   2. The package includes:

      - postgresql15-libs-15.8-1PGDG.rhel9.x86\_64
      - postgresql15-15.8-1PGDG.rhel9.x86\_64
      - postgresql15-server-15.8-1PGDG.rhel9.x86\_64
      - postgresql15-contrib-15.8-1PGDG.rhel9.x86\_64
2. Place the packages into Target server.
3. Perform the **TimeScale** package download on a server with internet access.

   ```
   mkdir timescale_offline && cd timescale_offline
   sudo apt update
   apt-get download timescaledb-2-postgresql-16 timescaledb-2-loader-postgresql-16
   ```
4. Place the packages into Target server.

## Installation Steps

1. Start by installing PostgreSQL as a separate instance from the metadata database.
2. Use the command below to install the package locally.

```
yum localinstall *
```

3. Initiate the database using below command.

```
/usr/bin/postgresql-15-setup initdb
```

4. Edit the data service path.

```
vi /usr/lib/systemd/system/postgresql-15.service
```

```
Environment=PGDATA=/opt/pgsql/15/data
```

5. Create a folder, grant an owner and setup the permission.

```
sudo mkdir -p /opt/pgsql/15/data
chown postgres:postgres -R /opt/pgsql
chmod -R 777 /data
chmod 777 /opt
```

6. Copy postgresql data/config that initiated

```
cp -avr /var/lib/pgsql/15/data/* /opt/pgsql/15/data
```

7. Configure the socket connection address and replication address.

![image-20260302-084544.png](https://blendata.atlassian.net/wiki/download/attachments/2977398792/image-20260302-084544.png?api=v2)

8. Edit postgresql configuration.

```
vi /opt/pgsql/15/data/postgresql.conf
```

![image-20260302-085138.png](https://blendata.atlassian.net/wiki/download/attachments/2977398792/image-20260302-085138.png?api=v2)![image-20260302-085229.png](https://blendata.atlassian.net/wiki/download/attachments/2977398792/image-20260302-085229.png?api=v2)

9. Start service postgresql.

```
systemctl start postgresql-15
```

10. Create a role with the password in for postgresql (only server 1)

```
sudo -u postgres psql -p 5433
```

```
CREATE ROLE replicator WITH SUPERUSER PASSWORD 'password';
ALTER ROLE replicator WITH LOGIN REPLICATION PASSWORD 'password';
ALTER USER postgres WITH PASSWORD 'password';
```

11. Then, proceed to the TimescaleDB installation.

## TimeScale Installation and Setting

1. Install on the offline server.

```
sudo dpkg -i *.deb
```

2. Tune the PostgresSQL instances for the TimescaleDB.

```
sudo timescaledb-tune
```

3. Restart the PostgreSQL to apply the timescaleDB extension.

```
systemctl restart postgresql
```

4. Login to the PostgreSQL as **postgres**.

```
sudo -u postgres psql
```

5. Set password for postgres account.

```
\password postgres
<password>
```

6. Create a timescale user.

```
create user timescale_user with password '<password>';
```

7. Create the timescale database.

```
create database timescale_db;
```

8. Grant all permissions to the timescale\_user.

```
GRANT ALL PRIVILEGES ON DATABASE timescale_db TO timescale_user;
```

9. Grant a public schema to the timescale\_user.

```
\c timescale_db
GRANT ALL ON SCHEMA public TO timescale_user;
```

10. Login with the timescale\_user.

```
psql -h localhost -U timescale_user -d timescale_db
<password>
```

11. Create an extension.

```
CREATE EXTENSION timescaledb;
```

12. Configure the Hera to apply the timescale extension.

```
create.access.log=true
default.access.log.retention.time= 7 day
postgres.host=<postgresql_ip>
postgres.port=5432
postgres.username=timescale_user
postgres.password=<password>
postgres.database=timescale_db
default.hypertable.chunk.interval= 1 day
```

- **create.access.log**: Set as true.
- **default.access.log.retention.time**: The default retention time applies to logs saved in the database. If a log exceeds the set retention time, it will be deleted.
- **postgres.host** : The host of the PostgresSQL IP address.
- **postgres.port** : The port of the PostgresSQL that is installed from above steps.
- **postgres.username**: The username of user that is created from above steps.
- **postgres.password**: The password of user that is created from above steps.
- **postgres.databas**e: The database name that is created from above steps.
- **default.hypertable.chunk.interval**: The properties that define the audit log table's partitioning into chunks. If set to 1 day, then the partition for the audit log is set to every day. If set to 2 days, then the audit log is set to partition every 2 days, so the chunk should not be separated too much, and the data inside the chunk should also not be too much. (The default is 1 day)

13. Restart Hera (Hera server).

```
cd /app/blendata/hera
bin/stop-hera.sh
bin/start-hera.sh
```

14. **Enable the audit log** on Blendata Enterprise by following the steps.
15. Open the Blendata Enterprise.
16. Import the PostgresSQL database into the Blendata Enterprise using the information from Audit Log setting steps.
17. Select “**Select Database Tables**“ option and click “**Next**“.

![postSQL.png](https://blendata.atlassian.net/wiki/download/attachments/2977398792/postSQL.png?api=v2)

18. Select the **public\_access\_log** table and set table name as “**access\_log**“.
19. Click “**Next**“ to complete the PostgreSQL table import.

![postSQL seelct table.png](https://blendata.atlassian.net/wiki/download/attachments/2977398792/postSQL%20seelct%20table.png?api=v2)

20. You can see the audit log from data preview option, Data Exploration menu, or create an table visualization to filter the fields.

![viz log.png](https://blendata.atlassian.net/wiki/download/attachments/2977398792/viz%20log.png?api=v2)

**public\_access\_log** is a database that stores all events or activities occurring in Blendata Enterprise, which consists of the following fields:

- **id**: Log id value.
- **created\_date**: The date when the action was performed.
- **username**: The username who performed an event or activity.
- **method**: The API method types including POST, GET, PUT, and DELETE.
- **end\_path**: The API urls.
- **response\_code**: The response code value.
- **request\_body**: The request body includes the parameter of the API. (Sensitive data, such as password value or API key in the request\_body, will be masked.)
- **error\_message**: The error message that display after running the API.

This audit log also has a housekeeping process configured in the settings. By default, logs are stored in the database for 7 days. Once a log exceeds the retention period, it is automatically deleted.

---

## Blendata CLI Installation

| Field | Value |
|---|---|
| **URL** | https://blendata.atlassian.net/wiki/spaces/bde460/pages/2982019074/Blendata+CLI+Installation |
| **Last modified** | 2026-03-02T09:42:36.377Z |
| **Breadcrumb** | Blendata Enterprise V.4.6.0 > Administrator User Manual > Post-Installation > Other Services for Blendata |

Blendata CLI is an application that use to connect to HERA Application via command line. Users need to install the Blendata CLI package as per below steps:

1. Download the **Blendata CLI** jar file. (internal link, which is available for Blendata team only)

link: <https://drive.google.com/drive/folders/13NaPfilLI94G0mMeYOdx4KCZG8rg4LIG?usp=drive_link>

2. Run the jar file using below commands.

```
blendata-cli/bin/bld-cli -u "{user}" -p "{password}" -H "{hera_ip}" -P "{hera_port}"
```

- **user**: fill in your user account.
- **password**: fill in your account password.
- **hera\_ip**: fill in the Hera IP address.
- **hera\_port**: fill in the Hera Port.

The super administrator role is not required to run this JAR file.

## Audit Log Installation

| Field | Value |
|---|---|
| **URL** | https://blendata.atlassian.net/wiki/spaces/bde460/pages/2995093505/Audit+Log+Installation |
| **Last modified** | 2026-03-17T13:40:36.113Z |
| **Breadcrumb** | (root) |

Audit logs allow system or super administrators to monitor all user activities in Blendata. Logs can be accessed via SQL commands or visualized in dashboards.

It is recommended to implement an audit log for monitoring all user activities. Please note that this requires a separate database to ensure data isolation and scalability.

Select an installation method based on your server's connectivity

- [Audit Log Installation (No Internet Access)](https://blendata.atlassian.net/wiki/x/CIB3sQ)
- [Audit Log Installation (With Internet Access)](https://blendata.atlassian.net/wiki/x/AQCqhw)

## SSO with Google

| Field | Value |
|---|---|
| **URL** | https://blendata.atlassian.net/wiki/spaces/bde460/pages/3012591617/SSO+with+Google |
| **Last modified** | 2026-03-16T06:05:38.748Z |
| **Breadcrumb** | Blendata Enterprise V.4.6.0 > Integration |

# How to Configure SSO with Google

## Step 1: Google Setting

1. Login to Admin console and go to **Apps** menu->Overview->Web and mobile apps.

![image-20260313-044105.png](https://blendata.atlassian.net/wiki/download/attachments/3012591617/image-20260313-044105.png?api=v2)

2. Click “**Add app**” button and select “**Add custom SAML app**”.

![image-20260313-045254.png](https://blendata.atlassian.net/wiki/download/attachments/3012591617/image-20260313-045254.png?api=v2)

3. Fill in the App Name field with '**Blendata**' and click 'Continue'

![image-20260313-050018.png](https://blendata.atlassian.net/wiki/download/attachments/3012591617/image-20260313-050018.png?api=v2)

4. Copy the SSO URL, Entity ID, and Certificate value for the next step.

![image-20260313-050059.png](https://blendata.atlassian.net/wiki/download/attachments/3012591617/image-20260313-050059.png?api=v2)

5. Enter ACS URL and Entity ID and press continue button.

   - **ACS URL**: Fill in the Blendata ACS url endpoint like <https://xxx.xxx.xxx.xxx:11010/auth/sso/login/callback>.
   - **Entity ID**: Copy the Entity ID value from step 4.

![](https://blendata.atlassian.net/wiki/download/attachments/3012591617/%E0%B8%81%E0%B8%B9%E0%B9%80%E0%B8%81%E0%B8%B4%E0%B9%89%E0%B8%A5.jpg?api=v2)

6. Press **FINISH** button.

![image-20260313-050801.png](https://blendata.atlassian.net/wiki/download/attachments/3012591617/image-20260313-050801.png?api=v2)

7. Access to Admin console and select **Directory** menu. Then, select the **Users** option and click **Add new user**.

![image-20260313-050959.png](https://blendata.atlassian.net/wiki/download/attachments/3012591617/image-20260313-050959.png?api=v2)

## **Step 2: SSO Management On Blendata Settings**

Administrators are allowed to access the SSO Management menu and manage user accounts.

1. Open Blendata Enterprise website and go to **SSO Management** menu.
2. Create a new SSO Configuration and fill in details below:

   - Enter the SSO configuration name.
   - In the **Entity ID** field, enter the value from **step 4**.
   - In the **Sing-on URL** field, enter an SSO url from **step 4**.
   - In the **Logout URL** field, can skip.
   - In the **ACS URL** field, enter a Blendata ACS url endpoint from step 5 like <https://xxx.xxx.xxx.xxx:11010/auth/sso/login/callback>.
   - Enter **Certificate** value from **Step 4**
   - Click “**Create**” button to save the new SSO configuration.

![image-20260313-090348.png](https://blendata.atlassian.net/wiki/download/attachments/3012591617/image-20260313-090348.png?api=v2)

## **Step 3: Create Users On Blendata**

You can [create a new user](https://blendata.atlassian.net/wiki/spaces/BDE470/pages/3044118601/User) or [import the user](https://blendata.atlassian.net/wiki/spaces/BDE470/pages/3044118601/User#3.-Import-Users) into the platform.

### Create a new user via Create New User option

1. Open Blendata website, go to menu User Management, and click **Create User → Create New User** button.

![image-20260313-061429.png](https://blendata.atlassian.net/wiki/download/attachments/3012591617/image-20260313-061429.png?api=v2)

2. Fill in the user information, select **Authentication Source** as “**SSO**” and select **User Source** drop down list, and click “**Create**” button.

![image-20260313-060900.png](https://blendata.atlassian.net/wiki/download/attachments/3012591617/image-20260313-060900.png?api=v2)

### Create a new user via Import Users option

1. Open Blendata website, go to menu User Management, and click **Create User -> Import Users** button.

![image-20260313-062348.png](https://blendata.atlassian.net/wiki/download/attachments/3012591617/image-20260313-062348.png?api=v2)

2. Click “**Download User Import Template File**” button.

![image-20260313-062311.png](https://blendata.atlassian.net/wiki/download/attachments/3012591617/image-20260313-062311.png?api=v2)

3. Enter a user information on template file.

Make sure the Google user's email address is set to column **username**.

4. Click **Open File for Import**, select **User Source** drop down list from configuration name on **Step 1**, and click “**Upload**” button.

![image-20260313-062311.png](https://blendata.atlassian.net/wiki/download/attachments/3012591617/image-20260313-062311.png?api=v2)

---

## Firewall Rules Setting for Data Source Connection

| Field | Value |
|---|---|
| **URL** | https://blendata.atlassian.net/wiki/spaces/bde460/pages/3021209621/Firewall+Rules+Setting+for+Data+Source+Connection |
| **Last modified** | 2026-03-19T13:21:15.616Z |
| **Breadcrumb** | Blendata Enterprise V.4.6.0 > Administrator User Manual > Post-Installation > Related Document |

# Firewall Rules Setting for Data Source Connection

To enable the Blendata platform to connect with essential data sources, you must configure the firewall rules accordingly. Each service requires specific firewall access to be opened, as detailed in the following table:

| **Source** | **HERA Service** | **Zeus Service** | **Spark Worker** | **Zeppelin Service** | **Airflow Service** |
| --- | --- | --- | --- | --- | --- |
| Database (RDBMS) |  | x | x | x |  |
| NO-SQL |  | x | x | x |  |
| File Path (HDFS,NFS,S3) |  | x | x | x |  |
| Kafka | x | x | x | x |  |
| ELK (Elastic Stack) |  | x | x | x |  |
| Source Collect from API | x | x | x | x |  |
| Google Sheet | x | x | x | x |  |

All services are mandatory except for the Zeppelin service, which is optional.

ELK includes Elasticsearch, Logstash, and Kibana.

## How to Enable Authentication for Spark Web UI

| Field | Value |
|---|---|
| **URL** | https://blendata.atlassian.net/wiki/spaces/bde460/pages/3095396357/How+to+Enable+Authentication+for+Spark+Web+UI |
| **Last modified** | 2026-05-06T08:42:11.769Z |
| **Breadcrumb** | Blendata Enterprise V.4.6.0 > Administrator User Manual > Related Documents |

If the Spark Web UI is accessible without authentication, please follow the steps below to configure access control.

## Requirements

- Apache Spark \*\*3.x\*\*
- Java 8+ (tested with JDK 8)

## Step 1: Rollback Preparation

Create backup copies of the existing configuration files.

## Step 2: Download Authentication Package

![(blue star)](https://blendata.atlassian.net/wiki/s/-2069522349/6452/eaae94c6a2affaf35e508f35b397601c3535ccda/_/images/icons/emoticons/72/1f4e6.png) [spark-ui-authen-1.2.0](https://drive.google.com/file/d/1iGhb-oL5AtDdOKb86anWecSSSW9wVw2w/view?usp=sharing)

## Step 3: Deploy the Package

1. Copy the built JAR to both Spark and Zeus.

   1. `{SPARK_HOME}/jars` Ensure the JAR is deployed to all Spark nodes, including Master and Worker nodes.
   2. `{ZEUS_HOME}/extra-lib`
2. Add Configuration to Update these files:

   1. `{SPARK_HOME}/conf/spark-defaults.conf`

      ```
      #### v1.2.0 and above
      ```properties
      spark.ui.filters=com.bld.spark.authen.filter.SparkFormAuthenExpiredTimeBlendataFilter
      spark.com.bld.spark.authen.filter.SparkFormAuthenExpiredTimeBlendataFilter.param.secretFile=<properties-path>/spark-ui-auth.properties
      spark.com.bld.spark.authen.filter.SparkFormAuthenExpiredTimeBlendataFilter.param.heraUri=<hera-uri e.g. https://localhost:11010>
      ```
      ```
   2. `{ZEUS_HOME}/conf/spark.conf`

      ```
      #### v1.2.0 and above
      ```properties
      spark.ui.filters=com.bld.spark.authen.filter.SparkFormAuthenExpiredTimeBlendataFilter
      spark.com.bld.spark.authen.filter.SparkFormAuthenExpiredTimeBlendataFilter.param.secretFile=<properties-path>/spark-ui-auth.properties
      spark.com.bld.spark.authen.filter.SparkFormAuthenExpiredTimeBlendataFilter.param.heraUri=<hera-uri e.g. https://localhost:11010>
      ```
      ```
3. Create Authentication Configuration File

   - Create the file.

     ```
     spark-ui-auth.properties
     ```
   - Add configuration in file follow this.

     ```
     ```properties
     username=admin
     password=password
     expiration-minute=1
     ```
     ```

     \*\*expiration-minute = Session expiration time in minutes.
4. Restart the services to apply the updated configuration

   - Spark Master
   - Spark Worker (if any worker nodes are available)
   - Zeus

- Ensure Spark is restarted after updating configurations.

- Tested on \*\*Apache Spark 3.x\*\* (standalone & cluster modes).

Reference Document: [Read me](https://drive.google.com/file/d/1nMHbExVlwro6pqmf-hzBieRkP0tJSRIl/view?usp=sharing)

---
